From text 'merrill2021hungryforfully pages 94-101'.

DJ Merrill. Hungry for fully automated design of embedded systems? Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt documents a benchmark suite of PCB designs (bm1–bm11) with varied challenges (narrow aspect ratios, through-hole-only boards, high pin-count modems, unusual footprints) and describes preprocessing to regularize human designs into KiCad format (conversion, removal of non-functional elements, removal of copper pours and manual routing for affected nets, replacing silk-screen with courtyard polygons, simplifying board outlines, locking connectors, adding net classes, and fixing DRC errors). Comparative results use three metrics (via count, total trace length, CPU time) across five placer/router combinations: manual/manual, manual/FreeRouting, our placer/FreeRouting, manual/our router, and fully automatic. Key observations: via counts are presented per configuration and depend strongly on router behavior and a layer-change-cost parameter for the A* router; FreeRouting tends to detour on the same layer while the authors’ router uses more vias; manual placements usually yield shorter trace length than automatic placements; certain designs (bm6 narrow boards, bm8 through-hole layout, bm11 high pin-count tight areas) are particularly challenging for automated placement/routing. The authors also modified FreeRouting for batch use and used KiCad as a recommended open benchmark format.

messages.content:
Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000023

From text 'jansen2023fromwordsto pages 4-5'.

Peter Alexander Jansen. From words to wires: generating functioning electronic devices from natural language descriptions. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt describes two benchmarks and evaluation setup used in From Words to Wires. PINS100 is a benchmark of 100 common electronic components (components range from 2 to 40 pins) used to evaluate models' knowledge of component pinouts. Evaluation uses two binary PASS@1-like metrics: a strict measure (all pins must be correct) and a permissive measure (only function-critical pins required). Models evaluated include instruction-tuned OpenAI CHATGPT (GPT-3.5-TURBO), GPT-4, and Anthropic CLAUDE (versions referenced). Reported PINS100 results: GPT-4 achieves the highest strict scoring performance, generating accurate pinouts for 74% of components; GPT-3.5 and CLAUDE-V1 achieve ~55% and 56% strict accuracy respectively. Under permissive scoring GPT-4 produces pinouts that include the most critical pins for 86% of components. MICRO25 is a 25-task benchmark aimed at Arduino microcontroller-driven device creation spanning input, protocols, output, sensors, and logic; models were prompted to output device specs (BOM, pinouts, schematics, code) in annotated JSON. The excerpt does not provide MICRO25 quantitative performance numbers for GPT-4.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'jansen2023fromwordsto pages 1-2'.

Peter Alexander Jansen. From words to wires: generating functioning electronic devices from natural language descriptions. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874. This article has 7 citations.

The paper introduces two evaluation suites for language-model-driven circuit design: PINS100 (assessing model knowledge of electrical components) and MICRO25 (evaluating a model’s capability to design common microcontroller circuits and Arduino ecosystem code involving I/O, sensors, motors, protocols, and logic). The authors frame the task as converting high-level textual descriptions into complete device designs, encompassing ideation, parts lists, electronic schematics, and microcontroller code, then moving to physical implementation. In their reported results, large models "such as GPT-4 and Claude-V1" achieved between 60% and 96% PASS@1 on generating full devices across these benchmarks. The work also presents six case studies (e.g., a radiation-powered random number generator, emoji keyboard, visible spectrometer) showing language models used as design assistants, and notes evaluation and practical implementation steps using rapid prototyping.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'jansen2023fromwordsto pages 7-9'.

Peter Alexander Jansen. From words to wires: generating functioning electronic devices from natural language descriptions. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874. This article has 7 citations.

The paper benchmarks language-model-based circuit/design generation on two tasks: PINS100 and MICRO25. On PINS100 the models show "moderate-to-high" proficiency at component-level knowledge. GPT-4 outperforms Claude-V1 on MICRO25, producing "25 fully-functional devices" and achieving near-perfect performance on that benchmark. In a more open, qualitative design setting (Experiment 3) GPT-4 can produce designs that nearly meet specifications for six complex devices, but these outputs typically require "moderate correction by domain experts" and manual fixes before physical construction. Major challenges include prompt sensitivity, the models' tendency to rely on obsolete or end-of-life electronic parts, use deprecated libraries, and poor generation of low-level device drivers (often hallucinating libraries or producing first-pass implementations that need extensive fixes). Manual evaluation (similar to Copilot-style workflows) is currently necessary because "the lack of electronic simulators with large libraries of simulated devices" blocks automated end-to-end testing. The authors note that iterative generation from core requirements to finer details can improve performance, and that task-specific fine-tuning or better simulators could address robustness issues.


---

## pqac-00000026

From text 'jansen2023fromwordsto pages 3-4'.

Peter Alexander Jansen. From words to wires: generating functioning electronic devices from natural language descriptions. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874. This article has 7 citations.

The paper (From Words to Wires) presents a workflow (WORDS2WIRES) that generates full device specifications from natural language: bill of materials, component pinouts, schematics (as netlists), and microcontroller code. It evaluates pinout generation accuracy on a 100-component benchmark (Pins100, reported in Table 1). According to Table 1, GPT-4 achieves 74% under strict (exact) pinout scoring and 86% under permissive scoring; for context, GPT-3.5 scored 55% strict / 74% permissive and Claude scored 56% strict / 72% permissive. The paper also uses a MICRO25 benchmark to assess single-shot generation of error-free devices (single-turn, strict binary success similar to PASS@1), though the excerpt does not provide numeric MICRO25 results for GPT-4. An example device in the paper was generated by GPT-4. The authors note that generating structured device specifications before code (and using larger model context windows) enables producing thousands-of-token specifications and helps condition downstream code and schematic generation.


---

## pqac-00000027

From text 'jansen2023fromwordsto pages 9-10'.

Peter Alexander Jansen. From words to wires: generating functioning electronic devices from natural language descriptions. ArXiv, May 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2305.14874. This article has 7 citations.

The excerpt reports that GPT-4 attains high generation accuracy in constrained settings (Experiment 2), and can produce simple electronic device designs much faster than students—often in minutes versus hours or days. In a more open, complex generation condition (Experiment 3), however, nearly all generated devices contained errors and required domain-expert correction. Of six open-generation devices, three (emoji keyboard, non-contact temperature sensor, ultrasonic glasses) were produced in essentially functional forms before adding extra requirements. Generated devices in this work are small in scope (typically fewer than 50 components, under 50 lines of code, and run on Arduino microcontrollers with ~2K memory). The authors note that successfully designing all devices in the MICRO25 benchmark would equal the performance of a strong undergraduate microcontroller-design student, implying current LLMs are not yet at that full benchmark capability. Additional limitations include manual physical construction, lack of simulation tools for automated evaluation, safety concerns, and the need for domain expert review for real-world or safety-critical use. The excerpt does not provide numeric results for Pins100 or full MICRO25 pass rates.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000028

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 3-5'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt summarizes how machine learning augments many stages of the EDA flow. It notes that logic synthesis relies on heuristics and that many ML methods are applied there. Placement and routing are strongly coupled, motivating "routing-aware" ML methods in physical design. Fabrication steps such as mask optimization and lithography simulation are challenging and have seen recent ML approaches. Verification and manufacturing testing also use ML for test-set optimization and complexity reduction. The survey frames ML taxonomically by learning paradigms (supervised, unsupervised, active, reinforcement) and by model families: classical ML (linear regression, RF, SVM, XGBoost) and deep models (CNN, RNN, GAN, DRL, GNN). It maps model strengths to data types (CNN for grid/2-D imagery, RNN for sequences, GNN for graphs) and highlights DRL for large decision spaces. For high-level synthesis (HLS), ML is used for fast/accurate result estimation, to refine design-space exploration algorithms, and to recast DSE as active learning. Overall, ML is presented as a data-driven supplement to analytical models that reduces cost/time and addresses non-optimal heuristic processes across placement, routing, synthesis, fabrication, verification, and testing.

## pqac-00000029

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 33-34'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt presents a taxonomy of ML functionality in EDA and specific methods applied to placement, routing, logic synthesis and physical design. Four high-level ML usages are described: Decision making for tool-chain and hyper-parameter selection (e.g., using DNNs for logic synthesis choices), Performance prediction (supervised/unsupervised models used for fast QoR estimation such as congestion prediction and hotspot detection), Black-box optimization for design-space exploration (active learning with surrogate models like GP and other predictive models), and Automated design (AI-driven tasks such as placement and analog device sizing). Common algorithms across tasks include Linear Regression, Random Forests, XGBoost, CNNs, SVMs, GANs, and domain-specific choices like RL and GCN for automated placement. The table maps tasks to algorithms and typical inputs/outputs (e.g., placement & routing using SVM/CNN/GAN with netlist or layout features to predict wire-length and congestion; physical implementation using SVM/CNN/XG-Boost for lithography hotspots, IR drop, and delay). Key findings emphasize replacing brute-force/empirical configuration with ML, using surrogate models for guided search, and active learning strategies to sample new design points.

## pqac-00000030

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 1-3'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The survey organizes ML for EDA work both by EDA stages (HLS, logic synthesis and physical design — placement and routing, mask synthesis, analog, testing/verification, etc.) and by a taxonomy of four ML roles: decision making in traditional tool flows, performance prediction, black-box optimization for design-space exploration (DSE), and automated design. Decision-making ML replaces empirical choices (tool chains, hyper-parameters) to speed workflows. Performance-prediction models estimate design quality from prior databases to avoid expensive synthesis runs. Black-box optimization uses predictive models plus sampling strategies to guide DSE. Recent deep learning advances, particularly reinforcement learning, enable more fully automated solutions for very large design spaces. The paper emphasizes ML’s potential to capture reusable patterns (avoiding repeated analysis), accelerate solutions for NP-complete EDA problems, and improve scalability. Most surveyed work is recent (within about five years), and the survey reviews methods across the EDA flow while also discussing ML perspectives and future trends.

## pqac-00000031

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 9-11'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt surveys ML applications across HLS, logic synthesis, placement, routing and other physical-design subproblems. For design-space exploration and HLS, supervised models (Lasso, Ridge, ANN, SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost), Gaussian Processes, and Graph Neural Networks are used for timing/resource/latency prediction, cross-platform prediction, and operation-delay estimation. Active learning and GP/RF are applied to reduce sample needs and to protect Pareto-optimal designs (ATNE uses RF to “minimize the risk of losing ‘good’ designs”). ML also augments optimization: STAGE learns promising starting points for simulated annealing; FSA uses decision trees to generate new samples; ML predicts hyper-parameter effects on solution quality and runtime to tune SA/GA/ACO. For logic synthesis, ML schedules traditional optimizers (LSOracle uses DNN), classifies synthesis flows with CNNs, and employs reinforcement learning (GCN, A2C) to guide transformations. Placement work (PADE) extracts/evaluates data-paths with SVM and NN and separates their placement; routing uses CNNs (RouteNet) for DRC/hotspot detection. The authors recommend public DSE benchmarks and customized models combining domain knowledge for further gains.

## pqac-00000032

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 30-33'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt highlights concrete ML applications across EDA stages and provides a functionality-based taxonomy. For power estimation at RTL, methods such as PCA, MLP and CNN are used (PRIMAL), achieving large speedups (PRIMAL: 50×) with modest error (<5%), while GNN-based GRANNITE leverages netlist structure to improve transferability and runtime (two orders of magnitude speedup, ~5.5% error). For combinatorial reasoning and verification, GNNs (e.g., NeuroSAT) and combinations of GNN feature extractors with reinforcement learning have been applied to learn heuristics and guide SAT solvers. Physical design/placement has been accelerated by reusing deep learning frameworks: DREAMPlace treats placement as analogous to NN training and achieves >40× speedups on GPUs. Auto-tuning of design flows uses collaborative filtering, tree-based models (XGBoost), and deep RL with unsupervised GNN features to recommend tool parameters. The authors group ML roles in EDA into four categories: decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design; ML is already used to select toolchains for logic synthesis and other synthesis tasks.

## pqac-00000033

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 42-44'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The provided excerpt is a bibliography from the ML-for-EDA survey covering many works across placement, routing, logic/RTL synthesis, physical design and adjacent tasks. It shows ML methods applied to prediction (RouteNet for routability, PowerNet/GridNet for IR-drop and power/EM prediction), optimization and control (GCN-RL for transistor sizing; placement works like PADE and "Keep it Straight"), generative design (WellGAN, LithoGAN, GAN-OPC for layout and mask optimization), verification and testing (neural-network-guided coverage-directed test generation), algorithm/parameter selection (SATzilla portfolio selection; FIST feature-importance sampling for flow parameter tuning), and acceleration (GPU-accelerated simulation, HLS timing prediction). Common model families include graph neural networks, convolutional nets, generative adversarial networks, reinforcement learning, tree-based and portfolio methods. Cross-cutting themes visible in the references are transferability of models across designs, using graph representations for circuits, ML-guided parameter tuning and flow selection, and applying ML both for fast prediction and for guiding optimization/generation in classical EDA flows.

## pqac-00000034

From text 'huang2021machinelearningfor pages 38-39'.

Guyue Huang, Jingbo Hu, Yifan He, Jialong Liu, Mingyuan Ma, Zhaoyang Shen, Juejian Wu, Yuanfan Xu, Hengrui Zhang, Kai Zhong, Xuefei Ning, Yuzhe Ma, Haoyu Yang, Bei Yu, Huazhong Yang, and Yu Wang. Machine learning for electronic design automation: a survey. Jun 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3451179, doi:10.1145/3451179. This article has 490 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt (references list) shows that machine learning techniques have been applied across many EDA tasks relevant to placement, routing, logic synthesis, and physical design. Examples include deep learning for logic optimization and timing signoff, learning-based floorplanning and local-search heuristics, prediction of IR drop, reinforcement learning for logic synthesis, neural-network migration for mask/ILT co-optimization, and CNN ensembles for layout hotspot detection. There are also works on learning combinatorial optimization over graphs and automated heuristic design for SAT solvers, indicating use of graph neural networks, supervised models, and heuristic/meta-learning. The references include theoretical foundations (e.g., multilayer feedforward networks as universal approximators) and cover applications such as surrogate modeling, prediction (timing, power, IR drop), algorithm-selection/heuristic learning, and replacing expensive checks with learned approximations. While the excerpt does not present the survey’s explicit taxonomy, the cited works imply common taxonomic axes: EDA stage (placement/floorplanning, routing, timing, power, mask/layout, verification), ML paradigm (supervised regression/classification, reinforcement learning, graph methods, ensembles), and use-case (prediction/surrogate, heuristic learning, optimization/auto-tuning).

## pqac-00000035

From text 'zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 8-10'.

Ruizhe Zhong, Xingbo Du, Shixiong Kai, Zhentao Tang, Siyuan Xu, Hui-Ling Zhen, Jianye Hao, Qiang Xu, Mingxuan Yuan, and Junchi Yan. Llm4eda: emerging progress in large language models for electronic design automation. Preprint, Jan 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2401.12224, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2401.12224. This article has 78 citations.

The excerpt discusses evaluation goals and metrics for generated RTL (syntax, functionality, and design quality such as PPA), and notes VerilogEval's use of a pass@k metric to quantify correctness of generated RTL. It surveys HDL verification and analysis applications of LLMs: domain-specific SFT datasets for bug summarization (ChipNeMo), automated code repair (RTLFixer) using RAG/ReAct-style feedback, and LLM-based assertion generation and checking to find RTL bugs and security issues. Frameworks like NSPG and DIVAS identify properties/CWEs from documentation and convert them into SystemVerilog Assertions. The paper covers dataset construction for fine-tuning LLMs on EDA artifacts and provides a table of backbones (e.g., RTLCoder, VerilogEval, ChatEDA) and model sizes used in recent works. In outlook, it frames logic synthesis as a sequential decision optimization problem (pre-mapping, technology mapping, post-mapping) and notes existing ML/optimization approaches—heuristics, Bayesian Optimization, and Reinforcement Learning—and proposes that LLMs can generate optimization sequences and arguments given HDL and PPA targets to iteratively improve QoR. Physical design is briefly mentioned but not detailed in this excerpt.

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-00)

| Tool Name | Vendor/Type | License/Open-Source | AI/Automation Features (component selection, schematic generation, auto-placement, autorouting, DFM/DRC, BOM/supply chain) | Scriptability/CI Suitability | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KiCad | KiCad project; desktop schematic/PCB suite | Open-source (GPL) | Strong automation but little built-in AI: Python scripting/plugins, Action Plugins, CLI exporters, IPC/API ecosystem, netlist/BOM generation, ERC/DRC, push-and-shove router, external autorouting via FreeRouting, library-table driven reuse; widely used as open benchmark format and automation backbone in research workflows (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Excellent for open-hardware CI: text-based files, Python automation, BOM/Gerber generation in pipelines, good Git friendliness; strongest fit for reproducible/open workflows | No first-party generative schematic or placement AI; FreeRouting integration is external and limited; complex constraint management and enterprise PLM/supply-chain tooling lag top commercial suites |
| Altium Designer / Altium 365 | Altium; enterprise PCB suite + cloud collaboration | Proprietary/commercial | Mature automation stack: ActiveBOM and supply-chain visibility, rules/constraints, interactive routing, xSignals/high-speed checks, cloud collaboration/review, MCAD co-design; emerging AI/copilot-style assistance in Altium 365/CoDesigner positioning around search, documentation and design assistance rather than fully automatic PCB generation | Moderate: scripts/APIs and cloud workflows exist, but CI is less open than code-first tools; version control better in Altium 365 than classic binary-file workflows | Proprietary stack; editability/version diffs weaker than plain-text/open formats; AI features are assistive and workflow-centric, not fully controllable generative placement/routing |
| Cadence Allegro / OrCAD X | Cadence; high-end PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Very strong automation: constraint-driven design, signal/power integrity integration, Allegro routing engines, topology planning, DFM/DRC, library/data management; AI/ML positioning via Allegro X AI/optimization engines for placement/routing guidance and productivity; strong manufacturing and enterprise flows | Good enterprise automation through Tcl/SKILL/APIs and batch flows, but less Git/CI-native than text/code-based ecosystems | Expensive and complex; strong automation still requires expert constraint setup; closed formats and licensing make open-hardware/CI workflows harder than KiCad/SKiDL-style stacks |
| Siemens Xpedition / PADS Professional | Siemens EDA; enterprise PCB suite | Proprietary/commercial | Constraint-driven layout, advanced routing, HyperLynx SI/PI analysis, DFM/manufacturing links, enterprise library management; strong automation in rule checking, design reuse, and verification; Siemens portfolio also connects to Valor-style manufacturing analysis in broader flow | Enterprise scripting/integration exists, but cloud-native CI friendliness is limited relative to open text-first tools | Powerful but heavyweight; AI is mostly optimization/analysis assist rather than transparent generative design; high barrier for small teams and open-hardware collaboration |
| Zuken CR-8000 | Zuken; enterprise system-level and PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Strong system/board co-design, 3D multi-board, harness/backplane integration, rule-driven placement and routing, advanced constraints, DFM/DRC/manufacturing preparation, parts/library governance; automation is rule-centric more than LLM/generative | Enterprise integrations exist, but not especially Git/CI-native; better suited to controlled corporate flows | Closed ecosystem; steep learning curve; limited visibility/editability for code-review-style hardware workflows; AI-native features less prominent than in newer startup tools |
| Autodesk Fusion Electronics / EAGLE | Autodesk; integrated ECAD/MCAD, cloud-connected | Proprietary/commercial (free tiers historically limited) | Good mainstream automation: schematic capture, push-and-shove routing, autorouter, MCAD integration in Fusion, library/BOM generation, cloud project sharing; useful for mechanical-electrical co-design more than AI-native generation | Some automation/export possible, but weaker scripting and CI ergonomics than KiCad/code-first stacks | EAGLE-to-Fusion transition created ecosystem uncertainty; less capable for advanced constraints than Cadence/Altium; limited built-in AI and weaker open workflow support |
| DipTrace | Novarm; mid-range desktop PCB tool | Proprietary/commercial | Traditional automation: schematic capture, component/footprint libraries, basic autorouter, DRC, 3D preview, manufacturing exports, batch output; limited BOM/library automation | Limited scripting and CI suitability; mostly GUI-centric workflow | Minimal AI/generative capability; weaker ecosystem/integration depth; less suited to large-team, high-constraint, or reproducible code-review workflows |
| EasyEDA / JLCPCB | EasyEDA + JLCPCB/LCSC cloud ecosystem; browser-based PCB CAD | Proprietary/cloud service with some free access | Strong practical automation for low-cost builds: browser CAD, integrated LCSC library/part search, BOM/CPL generation, direct JLCPCB ordering, online collaboration, basic autorouting/DRC, rapid component selection around in-stock catalog; adjacent AI-assisted part/design help is emerging in the ecosystem | Moderate for lightweight cloud collaboration, but weak for strict reproducible CI; exports available, yet workflow is platform-centric rather than code-native | Vendor lock-in to manufacturing ecosystem, limited advanced constraints, weaker scriptability/version control than KiCad/code-based stacks, and automation optimized for convenience over deep controllability (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) |


*Table: This table compares major schematic capture and PCB suites as of 2025, focusing on concrete automation and AI-adjacent capabilities, openness, and CI suitability. It is useful for distinguishing enterprise-optimized tools from open, scriptable workflows better suited to reproducible open-hardware development.*

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| Tool Name | Category | License | Primary Capability | AI/Generative Method | CI/Version Control Suitability | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Flux.ai | AI-native startup | Proprietary SaaS | Cloud schematic + PCB collaboration, reusable blocks, real-time checks/validation, some BOM/library workflows; vendor claims up to 10x faster design | AI-assisted design help and validation in browser; collaborative automation rather than full autonomous layout (pqac-00000008) | Moderate: cloud-native and collaborative, but less transparent than text-first/open file workflows for CI | Closed platform; limited low-level scriptability compared with code-first/open-source flows; AI assistance still requires human review for manufacturability and constraints (pqac-00000008) |
| JITX | AI-native startup / code-based | Proprietary commercial language/toolchain | Programmatic schematic/PCB generation, component selection, constraint solving, reusable generators, supply-chain/BOM optimization | Constraint/program synthesis over code-defined hardware; automation is generator-driven more than free-form GenAI (pqac-00000008) | High for enterprise workflows: code-native and amenable to version control; strong fit for generated hardware artifacts | Closed ecosystem and custom language; editability by non-programmers can be weaker than GUI EDA; final manufacturability still needs review (pqac-00000008) |
| Quilter | AI-native startup / open-source research tooling | Open-source research tool | Automated PCB placement/layout on small academic boards | RL-based PCB placement/layout; compared as open-source baseline in later work (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000017) | Moderate: open-source helps reproducibility; can be versioned and scripted in research settings | Runtime high; more suitable for small benchmarks (<~100 components); weaker scalability than newer hierarchical methods (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000017) |
| SnapMagic / SnapEDA | AI-native startup | Proprietary SaaS with downloadable CAD assets | Component search, symbol/footprint sourcing, design autocomplete, part substitution, BOM cost optimization | Vendor-described AI trained on datasheets; chat-like interface for design actions and replacement suggestions (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) | Moderate: integrates with mainstream EDA and helps library/BOM workflows, but core flow is SaaS-centric | Best at library/component/BOM assistance, not full controllable board synthesis; outputs still need engineering verification (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) |
| CELUS Design Platform | AI-native startup | Proprietary SaaS | Requirement-to-architecture capture, component selection, functional block/schematic generation, early BOM creation | Requirements-driven recommendation/generation over component and solution graphs | Moderate: useful early in flow, but not primarily a code-first CI tool | Typically stops before fully editable production PCB layout; downstream handoff/editability can be limiting |
| Diadem | AI-native startup | Proprietary | Analog/mixed-signal circuit design assistance, part choice, simulation-driven iteration | AI-assisted analog design optimization and recommendation | Low-Moderate: proprietary workflow; some automation, but less mature for open CI than code-first tools | Narrower scope (analog front-end design), not a general PCB CAD replacement; verification/simulation remain essential |
| Cofactr | AI-native startup / supply-chain | Proprietary SaaS | Electronics procurement, sourcing, inventory, compliance, BOM and procurement intelligence | AI/data-driven supply-chain matching and procurement optimization | Moderate: API/SaaS friendly for procurement workflows; complements CI rather than replacing EDA | Not a PCB authoring/layout tool; depends on external design/BOM quality |
| Allspice | AI-native startup / collaboration infrastructure | Proprietary SaaS | Git-based hardware review, diff, collaboration, design portals, CI hooks around EDA files | AI-assisted review/documentation is secondary; core value is Git-native hardware collaboration | High: among the best fits for version control, reviews, and CI around hardware files | Not a schematic/layout generator itself; depends on upstream CAD tool openness |
| DeepPCB / InstaDeep | AI-native research / startup effort | Research/proprietary mix | Routing/fanout automation, PCB physical-design optimization | Deep RL for routing/fanout/placement-style tasks; industrial/simple-board focus reported in literature (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000001) | Low-Moderate: promising algorithmically, but not yet a broadly adopted, fully open production workflow | Generalization and manufacturability remain difficult; literature notes success mainly on simpler 2-layer/smaller cases, though FanoutNet improved industrial fanout (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000001) |
| Google / Microsoft ML placement efforts | Research benchmark class | Research papers / internal tools | ML-driven placement/floorplanning guidance, mostly IC/chip not PCB | Deep RL and learned policy optimization for placement/floorplanning; informs PCB research direction (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000030) | Low direct CI relevance for PCB users; mainly conceptual/algorithmic influence | Mostly VLSI/IC rather than practical PCB CAD; difficult translation from chip floorplanning to manufacturable PCB layouts (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000030) |
| atopile | Code-based / open-source | Open-source | Python-like HDL for electronics, reusable modules, package/dependency management, netlist/BOM generation | No strong native AI; generation is code/compiler-driven | High: excellent fit for Git, reviews, reproducibility, and CI pipelines | Immature compared with mainstream GUI suites; board-layout handoff/editability still evolving |
| tscircuit | Code-based / open-source | Open-source | TypeScript/React-based electronic design, reusable components, programmatic PCB/schematic generation | No core AI; declarative/programmatic generation | High: excellent for Git/npm/CI workflows and software-style reuse | Young ecosystem; manufacturing and advanced PCB constraints less mature than incumbent EDA suites |
| SKiDL | Code-based / open-source | Open-source | Python schematic capture/netlist generation, reusable circuit definitions, BOM generation | No native AI; schematic generation by code abstraction | High: very scriptable and CI-friendly, especially with Python testing/tooling | Primarily schematic/netlist-centric; requires downstream PCB tools; limited native layout automation |
| Horizon EDA | Open-source EDA | Open-source | Schematic capture, PCB layout, robust library/pool management | No native AI; rule-driven conventional CAD | Moderate-High: open files and libraries help version control | Smaller ecosystem and less automation/AI than major commercial tools |
| gEDA | Open-source EDA | Open-source | Legacy schematic/netlist/simulation toolchain | No native AI; scriptable traditional UNIX-style tooling | Moderate-High: text/script friendly in places | Aging UX/ecosystem; weaker modern PCB and collaboration capabilities |
| Edea | Open-source utility | Open-source | Python manipulation/automation of KiCad project files and workflows | No native AI; automation via Python APIs | High: very suitable as glue for CI, checks, transformations, and code generation around KiCad | Not a full CAD front-end; depends on KiCad and surrounding tooling |
| PCBmodE | Code-based / open-source | Open-source | SVG- and Python-based PCB generation with high design programmability | No native AI; graphics/code-driven generation | High: source-text artifacts work well with Git and reproducible builds | Niche workflow; weaker support for advanced mainstream CAD constraints and enterprise flows |
| KiCad + scripting ecosystem (reference open workflow) | Open-source baseline | Open-source | Full schematic/PCB CAD, Python scripting, file-format automation, Gerber generation, third-party autorouting integration | Conventional automation via scripts/plugins rather than native GenAI; used in automated benchmark/manufacturing workflows (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Very High: among the best mainstream options for open hardware + CI because files, plugins, and automation are accessible | Native AI is limited; advanced autorouting/constraint automation trails top commercial suites; external tools often needed for full automation (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) |


*Table: This table compares AI-native EDA startups and code-based/open-source PCB ecosystems by capability, automation method, CI suitability, and limitations. It is useful for identifying which tools are best suited for open-hardware, scriptable, and CI-driven workflows versus closed AI-assisted SaaS workflows.*

function.arguments:
{"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a table of benchmark and case-study results from the literature on AI/ML-assisted PCB and EDA automation. Columns: Paper/Tool, Year, Task, Method, Benchmark/Dataset, Key Quantitative Result, Limitations/Notes. Include these rows:
1. Vassallo & Bajada 2024 (DATE) - PCB placement - RL (TD3/SAC) with adaptive rewards - Unseen circuit layouts - 17% (TD3) / 21% (SAC) post-routing wirelength reduction vs simulated annealing
2. Li et al. 2023 (AAAI, FanoutNet) - PCB fanout automation - Deep RL (PPO + CNN/attention) - Real industrial PCB benchmarks - 100% routability, 6.8% avg wirelength improvement  
3. Li et al. 2025 (IEEE TCAD, HiePlace) - Hierarchical PCB placement - Mathematical programming + dynamic prioritization - 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic) - 66% cost reduction, 7.7x speedup; 76.7-91.3% cost reduction vs Quilter
4. Jansen 2023 (arXiv, Words to Wires) - NL-to-circuit generation - GPT-4/Claude LLMs - Pins100 (100 components), Micro25 (25 Arduino tasks) - GPT-4: 74% strict / 86% permissive pinout accuracy; 60-96% Pass@1 on full device generation
5. Merrill 2021 (thesis) - Fully automated embedded PCB design - SA placement + A* routing - 11 open benchmark PCBs in KiCad format - Full automation demonstrated; some designs still challenge automated placement
6. Huang et al. 2021 (ACM TODAES survey) - ML for EDA survey - Multiple (RL, GNN, CNN, etc.) - Comprehensive review across stages - DREAMPlace 40x GPU speedup; PRIMAL 50x speedup with <5% error for power estimation
7. Zhong et al. 2024 (arXiv, LLM4EDA) - LLMs for EDA - LLM chatbots, HDL generation, verification - Survey of LLM applications - LLMs effective for HDL generation, chatbots, verification; physical design remains challenging
8. Zadeh & Elamien 2025 (IEEE Access) - Generative AI for analog IC - GNN, LLM, VAE - Analog circuit sizing tasks - AnalogCoder: 20/24 circuits automated; LLMs still struggle with precise numerical tasks"}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-02)

| Paper/Tool | Year | Task | Method | Benchmark/Dataset | Key Quantitative Result | Limitations/Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vassallo & Bajada (DATE) | 2024 | PCB placement | Reinforcement learning (TD3, SAC) with adaptive rewards | Unseen circuit layouts; compared against simulated-annealing PCB baseline | 17% (TD3) and 21% (SAC) average reduction in post-routing wirelength vs simulated annealing; faster convergence also reported (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) | Placement only; benchmark is limited relative to full industrial PCB flows; routing/manufacturability still downstream concerns (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) |
| Li et al. (FanoutNet, AAAI) | 2023 | PCB fanout automation | Deep RL using PPO + CNN + attention-based policy/value networks | Real-world industrial PCB benchmarks | 100% routability in all industrial cases; 6.8% average wirelength improvement vs prior methods (pqac-00000001) | Focused on fanout/pre-routing rather than full end-to-end PCB layout; depends on downstream router and industrial constraints encoding (pqac-00000001) |
| Li et al. (HiePlace, IEEE TCAD) | 2025 | Hierarchical PCB placement | Mathematical programming + dynamic prioritization + relaxation | 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic); Quilter comparison on academic cases | 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup overall; vs Quilter, 76.7%–91.3% cost reduction and runtime cuts up to ~99%+ on academic benchmarks (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000022) | Not an LLM/generative method; routability modeled via proxies; double-sided layouts noted as future work (pqac-00000022) |
| Jansen (Words to Wires, arXiv) | 2023 | Natural-language-to-circuit generation | GPT-4 / Claude-class LLMs generating BOMs, pinouts, schematics/netlists, and code | Pins100 (100 components), MICRO25 (25 Arduino-style device tasks) | GPT-4 reached 74% strict and 86% permissive pinout accuracy on Pins100; reported 60%–96% Pass@1 on full-device generation across benchmarks/cases (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) | Open-ended designs still required moderate expert correction; obsolete parts, hallucinated libraries, and lack of end-to-end simulation remain issues (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000027) |
| Merrill (thesis) | 2021 | Fully automated embedded PCB design | Simulated annealing placement + A*-based rip-up-and-reroute | 11 open PCB benchmarks in KiCad format | Demonstrated full automatic placement/routing workflow and benchmark suite; router prioritized connectivity and reportedly never produced LvS errors in cited evaluation description (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Hard boards still challenged automation; quantitative summary is less standardized than later ML papers; many advanced constraints omitted from benchmarks (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) |
| Huang et al. (ACM TODAES survey) | 2021 | ML for EDA survey | Survey of RL, GNN, CNN, XGBoost, SVM, GAN, DRL, etc. | Cross-stage review: HLS, logic synthesis, placement/routing, verification, manufacturing | DREAMPlace reported >40× GPU speedup for placement; PRIMAL reported 50× speedup with <5% error for RTL power estimation; GRANNITE reported ~2 orders-of-magnitude runtime speedup with ~5.5% error (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032) | Survey spans mostly IC/VLSI and broader EDA, not only PCB; results are drawn from multiple subfields and are not directly comparable head-to-head (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000032) |
| Zhong et al. (LLM4EDA, arXiv) | 2024 | LLMs for EDA | Survey of LLM chatbots, HDL/script generation, verification/analysis | Survey of recent LLM4EDA systems and datasets | Concludes LLMs are effective for HDL generation, chatbots, bug summarization/repair, and assertion generation; highlights pass@k-style evaluation such as VerilogEval for generated RTL (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000035) | Physical design remains difficult at full scale; hallucination, evaluation, and integration with downstream toolchains remain open problems (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000016) |
| Zadeh & Elamien (IEEE Access) | 2025 | Generative AI for analog IC design | Survey of GNNs, LLMs, VAEs, Bayesian optimization hybrids | Analog circuit sizing and schematic/layout-aware tasks | Reports AnalogCoder automated 20/24 circuits; summarizes reduced iterations and faster execution in LLM-assisted analog design pipelines (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000013) | LLMs still struggle with precise numerical reasoning, parasitic/PVT handling, and unseen topologies; many studies remain limited to simpler circuits (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000013) |


*Table: This table summarizes key benchmark and case-study results from 2021-2025 literature on AI/ML-assisted PCB and EDA automation. It highlights where measurable gains have been reported and where current methods still face limits in scale, verification, and manufacturability.*

function.arguments:
{"answer": "# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative, AI-Assisted, and Automated Tools for Electronic Design Automation, Schematic Capture, and PCB Design (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The electronics design automation (EDA) landscape is undergoing a rapid transformation driven by machine learning (ML), large language models (LLMs), and generative AI. A comprehensive 2021 survey catalogued ML applications across the entire EDA hierarchy—from high-level synthesis through placement, routing, verification, and manufacturing—identifying four principal ML roles: decision making, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000030). Since then, the field has accelerated with LLM-based code and schematic generation, reinforcement-learning (RL) placement engines, and cloud-native AI-assisted design platforms. This report provides a structured overview of the tool landscape as of 2025, organized into (a) mainstream EDA suites, (b) AI-native startups and products, (c) code-based and CI-friendly ecosystems, and (d) specific generative/automation capabilities and their limitations, with benchmark results where available.

---

## 2. Mainstream Schematic/PCB Design Suites and Their Automation/AI Features

The following table summarizes the major commercial and open-source EDA suites and their current AI/automation capabilities:

<artifact id="artifact-00"/>

### 2.1 KiCad and Its Scripting/IPC API

KiCad is the dominant open-source PCB design suite and serves as the de facto standard for open-hardware workflows. Its strengths lie in its Python scripting API, Action Plugin framework, command-line interface for Gerber/BOM generation, and text-based file formats amenable to Git version control. Research benchmarks frequently use KiCad as the standard format: Merrill (2021) developed an 11-design open benchmark suite in KiCad format for evaluating automated placement and routing, and Mamdouh et al. (2025) demonstrated automated Gerber and G-Code generation from KiCad, reporting a 13-fold improvement in routing efficiency compared to manual methods (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021). KiCad integrates with external autorouters such as FreeRouting and supports ERC/DRC checking. However, it lacks native AI-driven placement or generative schematic capabilities; these must be added through external tools or plugins.

### 2.2 Altium Designer and Altium 365 / Co-Designer AI

Altium Designer is a widely used commercial PCB suite featuring ActiveBOM for supply-chain visibility, xSignals for high-speed design, and strong rules/constraint management. Altium 365 adds cloud-based collaboration, design review, and MCAD co-design. Altium has been positioning AI-assisted features through its Co-Designer and copilot-style tools focused on documentation, component search, and design assistance, though full autonomous generative placement and routing remain nascent. JITX and SnapMagic are noted as compatible with Altium's ecosystem, reducing migration friction for teams adopting AI-native tools (pqac-00000008).

### 2.3 Cadence Allegro/OrCAD X and the Allegro AI/Optimality Engines

Cadence Allegro is an enterprise-grade PCB platform with a mature constraint-driven design methodology, signal/power integrity integration (via Sigrity), topology planning, and robust DFM/DRC. OrCAD X modernizes the interface while retaining Allegro's engine. Cadence has introduced AI/ML-powered optimization engines (marketed as Allegro X AI and Optimality Intelligence) that provide placement and routing guidance, congestion prediction, and productivity improvement. The broader Huang et al. (2021) survey documents how ML models—including CNNs for congestion/hotspot prediction and GNNs for netlists—are used across placement and routing stages, reflecting the direction Cadence and similar vendors are moving (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032).

### 2.4 Siemens Xpedition / PADS, Zuken CR-8000, Autodesk Fusion Electronics, DipTrace, EasyEDA

**Siemens Xpedition/PADS** provides constraint-driven layout, advanced routing, HyperLynx SI/PI, and Valor-based DFM/manufacturing analysis. Automation is primarily rule- and constraint-driven rather than generative-AI-native. **Zuken CR-8000** excels at system-level and multi-board co-design with 3D integration, strong constraints, and enterprise library governance. **Autodesk Fusion Electronics** (successor to EAGLE) offers cloud-connected ECAD/MCAD co-design with push-and-shove routing and a basic autorouter, but limited AI features. **DipTrace** provides a mid-range traditional workflow with basic autorouting and DRC. **EasyEDA** is a browser-based PCB tool tightly integrated with the JLCPCB/LCSC manufacturing and component ecosystem, offering rapid part selection from in-stock catalogs, basic autorouting, and direct ordering—optimized for convenience over deep constraint management.

---

## 3. AI-Native and Generative EDA Startups and Products

A new generation of tools is approaching PCB design from an AI-first or code-first perspective. The following table summarizes these tools:

<artifact id="artifact-01"/>

### 3.1 Flux.ai

Flux.ai is a cloud-based AI-assisted PCB design platform that leverages AI for real-time design validation, error detection and correction, design reuse, and team collaboration. The vendor claims up to 10× design acceleration (pqac-00000008). Its browser-based collaborative workflow is attractive for distributed teams but offers less low-level scriptability than code-first alternatives.

### 3.2 JITX

JITX uses a proprietary code-driven design language with constraint solvers and generators to programmatically produce schematics and PCB layouts. It integrates supply-chain awareness to suggest component replacements and cost optimizations, and generates fault log files for troubleshooting. JITX is compatible with Altium, Cadence, and Zuken ecosystems (pqac-00000008). Its code-native approach makes it well-suited for version control and CI, but its custom language creates a learning curve for non-programmers.

### 3.3 Quilter

Quilter is an RL-based automated PCB layout tool that has been used as an open-source baseline in academic benchmarks. Li et al. (2025) compared their HiePlace system against Quilter on academic PCB benchmarks (bm1–bm4), finding that HiePlace achieved 76.7%–91.3% cost reductions and runtime cuts of up to ~99% compared to Quilter, which had runtimes of 34–75 minutes per design (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018). Quilter supports pre-placement constraint settings but is more suitable for smaller designs with fewer than approximately 100 components (pqac-00000018).

### 3.4 DeepPCB / InstaDeep

InstaDeep (now part of the BioNTech ecosystem) explored deep RL for PCB routing. Literature notes that AI-driven routing currently works well primarily on simpler 2-layer boards with fewer than 150 connection pairs (pqac-00000003). Li et al. (2023) at AAAI demonstrated FanoutNet, a deep RL method for PCB fanout automation using PPO with CNN and attention networks, achieving 100% routability on all industrial cases and 6.8% average wirelength improvement over state-of-the-art methods (pqac-00000001).

### 3.5 SnapMagic/SnapEDA

SnapMagic provides an AI-powered, conversational interface trained on component datasheets. It can autocomplete designs, recommend part substitutions based on local market availability and cost, and enact high-level requests such as "Add an MCU with 2 SPI ports and USB 2.0 and connect the USB port to a USB-C connector" (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008). It integrates with mainstream EDA tools and excels at library, component, and BOM assistance, though it is not a full autonomous board synthesis tool.

### 3.6 CELUS, Diadem, Cofactr, Allspice

**CELUS Design Platform** takes a requirements-driven approach, enabling engineers to specify functional requirements that are then mapped to component selections and functional block/schematic generation, producing early BOM and architecture outputs. **Diadem** focuses on AI-assisted analog/mixed-signal circuit design optimization. **Cofactr** provides AI-driven supply-chain intelligence, procurement optimization, and inventory management as a complement to EDA tools. **Allspice** provides Git-based hardware version control with visual diff, design review portals, and CI hooks for EDA files—among the best-fitting tools for code-review-style hardware collaboration workflows.

### 3.7 Google/Microsoft ML Placement Efforts

Google's landmark work by Mirhoseini et al. (published in Nature, 2021) framed chip floorplanning as a deep RL problem and demonstrated that RL agents could generate placements comparable to or better than human experts for Google's tensor processing units. This work, while focused on VLSI/IC rather than PCB, has strongly influenced the broader field of automated placement research (pqac-00000003, pqac-00000028). The DREAMPlace framework treats placement as analogous to neural network training, achieving over 40× speedups on GPUs (pqac-00000032). Translation from chip floorplanning to manufacturable PCB layouts remains an open challenge.

---

## 4. Cloud/CI-Friendly and Code-Based Hardware Description Ecosystems

For open-hardware and CI-driven workflows, a critical requirement is that design files be text-based, scriptable, and version-controllable. Several ecosystems address this:

**atopile** is a Python-like hardware description language (HDL) for electronics with a pip-style package manager, enabling reusable circuit modules, dependency management, and netlist/BOM generation from code. Its text-first approach makes it excellent for Git workflows, CI pipelines, and reproducible builds, though its ecosystem is still maturing relative to GUI-based suites.

**tscircuit** brings electronic design into the TypeScript/React ecosystem, using npm-style packages and declarative/programmatic component definitions to generate schematics and PCB layouts. It is highly suited for software-engineer-friendly hardware development and CI integration.

**SKiDL** is a Python library that allows schematic capture and netlist generation entirely in code, with reusable circuit definitions and BOM generation. It is very scriptable and CI-friendly but requires downstream PCB layout tools (such as KiCad) for physical design.

**Horizon EDA** is a fully open-source EDA tool with a robust pool-based library management system. **gEDA** is a legacy open-source UNIX-style EDA suite with text/script-friendly formats. **Edea** is a Python library for programmatic manipulation of KiCad project files, useful as glue for CI pipelines, automated checks, and transformations. **PCBmodE** is a niche SVG- and Python-based PCB design tool offering high design programmability with source-text artifacts suitable for Git.

KiCad itself, combined with its Python scripting ecosystem, FreeRouting integration, and open file formats, remains the strongest mainstream option for open-hardware CI workflows. Research work has used KiCad as a recommended open benchmark format for PCB design evaluation (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021).

---

## 5. Generative/Automation Capabilities and Limitations

### 5.1 Component Selection and BOM Optimization

SnapMagic and JITX provide the most mature AI-assisted component selection capabilities, with SnapMagic offering datasheet-trained recommendations and cost optimization based on market availability, and JITX integrating supply-chain awareness into its code-driven generators (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008). CELUS automates early-stage component selection from functional requirements. LLMs have also been explored for component selection, with generative AI capable of producing bill-of-materials from natural language specifications (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000015).

### 5.2 Schematic Generation

LLMs have demonstrated a previously unknown capability for electronic circuit design from textual descriptions. Jansen (2023) introduced the Pins100 and MICRO25 benchmarks and showed that GPT-4 achieved 74% strict accuracy on component pinout generation (86% permissive) and between 60%–96% Pass@1 on generating full functioning devices including BOMs, schematics, and microcontroller code (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026). However, complex open-ended designs still require moderate expert correction, and models tend to use obsolete parts and hallucinate libraries (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000027). LLMs can generate HDL code (Verilog) for digital circuits, with domain-specific fine-tuning improving results; the LLM4EDA survey documents applications in HDL generation, verification, and analysis across the EDA flow (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000014). For analog IC design, generative AI methods including LLMs, GNNs, and VAEs have been applied to circuit sizing, with AnalogCoder automating 20 of 24 circuit tasks (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000013). Krishnamoorthy (2025) describes workflows where LLMs generate component-level schematics from circuit topology, with outputs that are editable and exportable to board layout tools (pqac-00000012, pqac-00000015).

### 5.3 Auto Placement

Reinforcement learning has emerged as the leading approach for automated PCB placement. Vassallo and Bajada (2024) demonstrated that RL agents trained with SAC and TD3 algorithms and adaptive reward signals achieved average post-routing wirelength reductions of 21% (SAC) and 17% (TD3) compared to simulated annealing baselines on unseen circuit layouts (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002). Li et al. (2025) presented HiePlace, a hierarchical mathematical-programming approach achieving 66% cost reduction with 7.7× speedup on 19 PCB benchmarks, and 76.7%–91.3% cost reduction versus the RL-based Quilter tool on academic benchmarks (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000022). At the IC level, DREAMPlace achieved over 40× GPU speedup for analytical placement (pqac-00000032).

### 5.4 Autorouting

FanoutNet (Li et al., 2023) applied deep RL (PPO with CNN and attention networks) to PCB fanout automation—a pre-routing stage—achieving 100% routability on all industrial test cases and 6.8% average wirelength improvement (pqac-00000001). Traditional approaches including A*-based rip-up-and-reroute remain common in practice, as demonstrated in the Merrill (2021) benchmark suite where the router prioritized connectivity and never produced logical-vs-schematic errors (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000019). AI-driven routing currently works best on simpler boards; production-scale complex multi-layer routing with differential pairs and impedance matching remains challenging for fully automated methods (pqac-00000003).

### 5.5 DFM/DRC

ML-based DFM and DRC checking is advancing through CNN-based hotspot detection, congestion prediction, and IR-drop estimation. The Huang et al. (2021) survey documents RouteNet for DRC/routability prediction, PowerNet/GridNet for power/EM prediction, and PRIMAL for RTL power estimation achieving 50× speedup with less than 5% error (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033). Siemens' Valor ecosystem and Cadence's Sigrity tools incorporate manufacturing analysis, though these are primarily rule- and simulation-driven rather than generative-AI-based.

### 5.6 Key Limitations

Across all tools and methods, several cross-cutting limitations persist:

- **Manufacturability**: AI-generated placements and routes may not respect the full set of manufacturing constraints (impedance, thermal, creepage) that production boards require. Routability is often modeled via proxies like wirelength and net crossings rather than comprehensive DFM rules (pqac-00000022).
- **Editability and controllability**: LLM-generated designs often require moderate expert correction, and proprietary AI tools may produce outputs that are difficult to manually refine or override (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000027).
- **Scalability**: RL-based placement methods struggle with large industrial boards (>100–200 components), and LLM-based approaches face context-window and consistency limitations for complex multi-stage designs (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000016).
- **Verification**: The lack of electronic simulators with large component libraries blocks automated end-to-end testing of generated designs; manual verification remains necessary for safety-critical applications (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000027).
- **Version control and CI**: Binary file formats in commercial tools (Altium, Cadence, Siemens) hinder Git-based workflows; only open-source tools (KiCad, atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL) and dedicated platforms (Allspice) provide first-class CI support.

---

## 6. Benchmark and Case-Study Results

The following table consolidates key quantitative results from recent literature:

<artifact id="artifact-02"/>

---

## 7. Recommendations for Open-Hardware, CI-Driven Workflows

For teams prioritizing open-source, scriptable, CI-driven workflows, the most suitable stack combines:

1. **KiCad** as the primary schematic/PCB design tool, leveraging its Python scripting API, text-based file formats, and open benchmark ecosystem (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021).
2. **Code-based HDLs** such as **atopile**, **tscircuit**, or **SKiDL** for programmatic circuit definition, reuse, and Git-native version control.
3. **Edea** for Python-based automation and manipulation of KiCad files in CI pipelines.
4. **Allspice** for Git-based hardware review, visual diff, and CI hooks.
5. **SnapMagic** or **JITX** for AI-assisted component selection and BOM optimization, with outputs compatible with KiCad/Altium/Cadence.
6. External autorouters (FreeRouting, or emerging RL-based tools) for routing automation, with manual review for production boards.

This stack preserves full traceability, editability, and reproducibility while incorporating AI assistance where it provides the most value—component selection, early schematic generation, and BOM optimization—while keeping human engineers in control of final placement, routing, and manufacturing preparation.

---

## 8. Conclusion

The EDA landscape in 2025 is characterized by a convergence of traditional constraint-driven automation with emerging ML/AI capabilities. Reinforcement learning has demonstrated measurable improvements in PCB placement (17–21% wirelength reduction) and fanout automation (100% routability with 6.8% wirelength gains) (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001, pqac-00000002). LLMs can generate functioning circuit designs from natural language with 60–96% Pass@1 on constrained benchmarks (pqac-00000024), and hierarchical mathematical approaches like HiePlace dramatically outperform earlier RL tools on placement cost and runtime (pqac-00000017). However, full end-to-end automated PCB design—from requirements through manufacturable Gerber files—remains an open challenge. The most practical near-term approach combines open, scriptable tools for reproducibility and CI with targeted AI assistance for component selection, early design exploration, and validation, while maintaining human oversight for final design decisions and manufacturing preparation."}
