contexts.context: The excerpt identifies the primary ML/AI methods applied to PCB placement and routing as deep reinforcement learning (DRL) combined with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), citing Mirhoseini and Goldie et al.'s "Chip Placement with Deep Reinforcement Learning" as a promising approach that automated component placement under certain conditions. It also notes industry work on AI-driven routing (e.g., InstaDeep). Classical auto-routers still primarily use heuristics and Design Rule Checks (DRCs). The text emphasizes the fundamental hardness: routing maps to NP-Complete problems (compared to Traveling Salesman), and placement is harder because it requires repeated routings during optimization. Regarding benchmarks, the excerpt provides limited quantitative results: it states that AI-driven routing currently works well only on simple 2-layer boards with fewer than 150 connection pairs. No production-scale placement benchmark numbers or detailed performance metrics for DRL+GNN placement are given in these pages.
contexts.question: What are the key ML/AI methods used for PCB placement and routing automation, and what benchmark results have been achieved?

snippets: With recent advancements in Machine Learning (ML) and Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), PCB design is on the verge of a much needed renaissance.
This thesis explored a promising method for placement automation proposed by Mirhoseini and Goldie et al.  in "Chip Placement with Deep Reinforcement Learning" [6].
Using deep reinforcement learning and powerful GNN techniques, the authors automated the component placement step of chip design under certain conditions.
Lucky, routing has seen a lot of excitement in recent years with companies like InstaDeep [1] using Artificial Intelligence (AI) to drive routing algorithms.
AI driven routing algorithms are still plagued by complexity, only working well on simple 2 layer boards with fewer than 150 connection pairs.
optimal PCB routing is a notorious example of the longstanding NP-Complete Traveling Salesman problem.
This thesis focuses on PCB component placement, the partner operation to routing.
Where PCB routing can be considered successful upon the completion of all connections, optimal PCB placement relies on a series of complete routings at each step in its optimization.
forces most auto placement algorithms to rely on heuristic

contexts.context: This paper (FanoutNet) frames PCB fanout as a Markov Decision Process and applies deep reinforcement learning to automate fanout, a pre-routing stage that greatly affects routability. Key ML components: convolutional neural networks (CNN) combined with an attention-based network to encode PCB representations and to produce both a policy model and a value model; Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for policy updates; and formulation as an MDP to enable RL training. Input representations combine a structured feature (a sequence encoding netlist info and partially generated fanout: pin locations, pin connections and current fanout locations) and a spatial feature (a rasterized multi-channel binary image of the PCB). The system also includes a Multi-Constraint Router (MCR) to verify fanout quality and final routing under industrial constraints. The authors report results on real industrial PCB benchmarks: 100% routability in all tested industrial cases and an average wire-length improvement of 6.8% compared with state-of-the-art methods. They contrast this approach with prior BGA escape-routing methods, which are unsuitable for general PCB fanout.
contexts.question: What are the key ML/AI methods used for PCB placement and routing automation, and what benchmark results have been achieved?

contexts.context: Excerpted text notes that contemporary EDA tools and research aim to automate repetitive PCB tasks—routing, component placement, verification—and assist designers rather than fully replace them. KiCad was used in the project to auto-generate Gerber and G-Code, yielding a reported 13-fold routing-efficiency improvement versus manual methods. The excerpt names commercial/AI-assisted tools (SnapMagic, Flux AI, JITX) as examples: SnapMagic is described in detail — it is trained on datasheets, can auto-complete designs, recommend part replacements/cost optimizations, and supports a chat-like interface to request actions (example: “Add an MCU with 2 SPI ports and USB 2.0 ...”). More generally, AI in EDA is positioned for tasks like design verification (checking component compatibility) and for CNC/path-planning optimization using algorithms such as genetic algorithms, ANNs, artificial immune systems, ant colony optimization, and particle swarm optimization. The overall framing is that AI-enabled commercial tools currently accelerate and simplify repetitive design steps and verification, while final decision-making remains with human designers.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and features of Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting API, Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, and other commercial EDA tools for automated PCB design?

contexts.context: The excerpt documents an automated PCB workflow built around KiCad and FlatCAM. KiCad is used to create schematics and PCBs and to generate Gerber/drill files via its scripting console; the project uses a component dataset (BJTs, FETs, amplifiers, with max currents and availability) to automatically determine minimum track width and guide component selection. Component placement and routing are automated (FreeRouting plugin), with auto-routing reported to be more than 13× faster than manual routing in this study. FlatCAM processes Gerber files into machine G-code, offering an interface to import/edit Gerber and drill files, select milling tips, set clearance/feed-rate, and perform isolation routing. Batch G-code generation is automated using a TCL script. The workflow includes continuity testing and practical milling trials (two milling tips) to validate manufacturability. The excerpt contains concrete capabilities for KiCad scripting and FlatCAM automation but does not provide information about Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, or other commercial AI EDA tool features.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes high-level limitations and a roadmap for automated PCB design rather than detailing specific commercial products. Two primary limits are cited: (1) library support — creating large, validated component libraries requires substantial engineer-hours — and (2) tool capability — contemporary commercial PCB tools can route moderately complex boards when given manual placement and routing constraints, but they are not designed for full automation and are closed-source. Open-source tools are modifiable but currently less capable for complex routing and not built for no-human-in-the-loop operation. The author presents an open, fully-automated PCB layout framework and proposes benchmarks (real designs with 19–57 components, 15–99 nets) and evaluation metrics to bridge research and industry. A future vision expects machine-readable electrical constraints, PCB HDLs to replace graphical schematics, and fully automated layout workflows; achieving this requires focused open-source efforts. The excerpt briefly notes Gepetto creates higher-complexity designs using pre-routed blocks and human-driven routing, indicating hybrid approaches currently exist.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and features of Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting API, Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, and other commercial EDA tools for automated PCB design?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes an open-source, fully-automated PCB layout framework that provides modular stages (shared design database, placer, autorouter, and design rule checker). The shared database can read/write KiCad (.kicad_pcb) files, expose C++ and Python APIs, export partial layouts for manual inspection in KiCad, and produce Gerber files for manufacturers. The framework’s placer uses a simulated annealing algorithm optimizing overlap penalties, unrouted air‑wire lengths, and a routing congestion prediction metric; the KiCad autoplacer is also supported but requires the KiCad GUI and focuses on minimizing air-wire length. The text emphasizes why VLSI tools are not directly applicable to PCBs: PCBs have far fewer components, arbitrary polygonal (and sometimes non-convex or disconnected) component shapes, two-sided/3D placement and mirroring, free rotation (including 45°), and routing differences (large vias, free-angle routing, and layer-count cost sensitivity). This motivates bespoke placement/routing algorithms and an API-driven, push-button flow that allows interchangeability of stages and manual intervention when desired.

snippets: Our framework is unique in being both open-source and fully-automated.
We are releasing the following modules as part of our framework:
shared design database
placer
autorouter
design rule checker (DRC)
the database, exposes both a C++ and Python API.
The database can read and write to the KiCad PCB layout format (.kicad pcb)
KiCad’s autoplacer attempts to minimize air-wire length, with a preference for compacting components into the lower-right corner of the board.

Our placer: We use a simulated annealing (SA) algorithm to find a placement that is optimized with regards to the sum of an overlap penalization term, unrouted air-wire lengths, and a routing congestion prediction metric[SJ07].
Upon completion of the layout, Gerber files can be generated from KiCad PCB files for multiple manufactures.
PCB components may have complex polygonal shapes, as opposed to the rectilinear cells of IC design.
PCB components may be placed on either the top of the bottom of the PCB.
PCB components may be freely rotated during placement, and 45-degree rotations are not uncommon in PCB layouts.
PCB designs contain orders of magnitude fewer components than VLSI.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes KiCad's Scripting Console: a Python API accessible from both the Schematic and PCB editors that automates repetitive EDA tasks and enables custom tools and reports. Key capabilities include automated component placement, netlist generation, design rule checks (example: DRC for tracks narrower than 2 mm with warnings), and programmatic board manipulation via the pcbnew module (reading the board, identifying components, adjusting positions, updating the PCB view). The project also automates BOM handling—generating a BOM CSV and producing enriched PDF reports (including data such as maximum currents, orientation, positioning, and local stock availability). A component dataset (BJTs, FETs, amplifiers, gates, comparators) is used to determine track widths and map footprints to physical components; users can create custom symbols/footprints and run ERC checks. The authors note potential gains from integrating machine learning for improved placement, path/drill optimization, and continuous design optimization. The excerpt does not provide details about Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, or other commercial EDA tools.

snippets: The KiCad Scripting Console represents a significant advancement in Electronic Design Automation (EDA), as it not only automates repetitive tasks but also enhances the functionality of KiCad by enabling the generation of custom reports and the development of new tools.
Accessible from both the Schematic Editor and PCB Editor, the scripting console provides a robust Python API that allows users to manipulate KiCad projects, schematics, PCBs, and libraries.
The KiCad Scripting Console can automate various repetitive tasks, including component placement, netlist generation, and design rule checks.
Among the most critical modules within the KiCad Scripting Console is **pcbnew**, which encompasses all functions available in the KiCad PCB editor

For instance, the following snippet reads the current board, identifies its components or modules by their reference designators, adjusts their positions relative to the origin, and updates the PCB view accordingly.
it generates the netlist and conducts Design Rule Checks (DRC) for tracks narrower than 2 mm, issuing a warning message to the user.
this project incorporates a function to generate a PDF report from a Bill of Materials (BOM) CSV file produced by KiCad

A comprehensive dataset comprising components such as BJTs, FETs, amplifiers, gates, and comparators has been developed for this project and is utilized as a reference for determining track widths based on input currents.
In addition, the integration of machine learning techniques into this framework could further enhance the design and optimization processes.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents an automation pipeline built around KiCAD, FlatCAM and G-code generation: an automated TCL script was developed to read Gerbers, process them, and generate G-Codes, and additional automation was implemented via PowerShell and Python scripts (the PowerShell script uses built-in functions to send keyboard keys and mouse clicks). The workflow includes establishing input/output directories, configuring physical parameters (spindle rate, z-cut depth, XY and Z feed rates, layers), reading Gerbers to extract design data, and generating Gerber files and drill files via separate functions. FlatCAM already applies optimization techniques (embedded from version 8.0), and the authors note the scripted G-codes currently need further optimization compared with FlatCAM outputs (automated G-code: 7181 lines vs manual: 4776 lines). The work also includes a dataset of over 1800 KiCAD components with maximum current and local availability to inform trace width selection and suggest alternatives. Automation reduced the set of required tools a designer must master to mainly the schematic editor and a G-code editor.

contexts.context: The excerpt does not describe vendor-specific feature lists for Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting API, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, or other named products in detail. Instead it describes general capabilities and design-flow considerations for automated schematic-to-board workflows that underpin such tools: mapping higher-level representations (e.g., component-functionality level code) to specific vendor parts using BOM preferences and optimization objectives (cost, size, part tag, guidelines, customer preference, carbon footprint); support for modular reuse across platforms by selecting vendor components; schematic placement priorities (visual readability over pure wirelength minimization), reliance on component dimensions and pin locations, alignment of pins, rotations/mirroring, multi-page offpage connectors, and creating editable generated designs that can be imported into layout editors. The excerpt also lists or links several commercial/online tools and vendors (deepPCB, Quilter, Flux.ai, CircuitMind, Cadence, Siemens EDA) but provides no detailed per-tool capability matrix. These general points indicate the typical automated-EDA feature set (BOM-aware part mapping, placement/wiring heuristics, editable outputs, multi-page handling, and modular reuse) one should expect when comparing Altium, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, and similar offerings.

Some form of modular approach could also be enabled to reuse a specific functionality onto multiple platforms and designs by merely choosing the right vendor components preferred for any given platform.
Once the schematics design is generated to represent a given circuit topology, it needs to be placed in a manner that a design engineer would manually create and review.

placement algorithms primarily rely on minimizing wirelength of the overall design. However in the case of schematics design, there is more emphasis on placing and wiring the designs in a visually understandable fashion.
The data containing component dimensions as well as the pin locations are important to enable proper relative placements as well as the wiring between the pins.
We start with a initial component with most connections to other components in the design and then place the other candidate blocks in a way that the connected pins are aligned.
Any required rotations and mirroring are evaluated and committed, to align pins connected to same net.
In the event that additional pages need to be used, offpage (page specific I/Os) connectors will be created and wires routed accordingly.
The Design thus generated is made editable for any required user corrections and updates. The design is then ready for board layout design, and it can be imported into layout editors.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes several AI-enabled and automation-focused PCB design tools. SnapMagic provides an interactive, conversational interface that can autocomplete designs from datasheets and enact high-level requests (example: “Add an MCU with 2 SPI ports and USB 2.0 and connect the USB port to a USB-C connector”), and it recommends part substitutions to lower BOM cost based on local market availability. Flux.ai is presented as an open cloud-based PCB tool that leverages AI to accelerate design (claimed up to 10x), automate repetitive tasks, reuse designs, support team synchronization, and perform real-time validation to detect and correct errors. JITX uses simple code-driven design flows, offers testing/validation with generated fault log files for troubleshooting, and integrates supply-chain awareness to suggest component replacements and cost optimizations. The excerpt also notes that JITX and SnapMagic (co-parent) are compatible with established EDA tools like Altium, Cadence, and Zuken, reducing migration needs. KiCAD is mentioned as being used to design a simplified PCB in the hardware tests (with FlatCAM and G-Code tools). The excerpt does not provide details on Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting API specifics, Quilter, CELUS, or other commercial EDA feature sets beyond the tools above.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities and features of Altium Designer AI, Cadence Allegro AI, KiCad scripting API, Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, CELUS, and other commercial EDA tools for automated PCB design?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes multiple capabilities of LLMs and generative AI in EDA. LLM-based engineering assistant chatbots can help engineers access domain knowledge, answer questions, and accelerate design workflows, though they risk hallucination and thus benefit from mitigation techniques like retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) and domain-specific retrieval fine-tuning (as in ChipNeMo). Industry tools (e.g., RapidGPT) act as AI pair-designers for FPGA engineers, offering conversational code assistance. LLMs are effective at translating natural-language specifications into HDL (e.g., Verilog) and other scripts, which can reduce human errors and speed translation. End-to-end automation is being explored: ChatEDA targets automation across RTL-to-GDSII flows by splitting tasks into planning, script generation, and execution, treating natural language as input and generating executable EDA tool scripts. The broader research areas also include evaluation of generated code and verification/analysis, indicating LLMs are applied not only to generation but to validation and verification stages of the design flow.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

contexts.context: The excerpt describes multiple capabilities of LLMs and generative AI applied to EDA. LLMs can act as interactive assistant chatbots for Q&A to speed knowledge acquisition. They can perform HDL and script generation: given language-format specifications and requirements, LLMs can generate RTL code and EDA control scripts, automating parts of circuit design and reducing manual coding. Evaluation of generated code is an open problem, with syntax correctness, functional equivalence, and EDA metrics like PPA and security requiring new frameworks. LLMs are also useful for verification and analysis (bug detection and fixing, code summarization, security checking) and have shown aptitude in assertion-based verification. For higher-level design flows, LLMs may generate logic-synthesis optimization sequences from HDL and prompts. Physical design (placement and routing) is currently challenging at full scale, but techniques such as graph partitioning and clustering can make LLM application feasible; LLMs might also optimize modules or serve as reward models for RL agents. The paper envisions an EDA-guided “large circuit model” trained on text and multi-modal data (specs, HLS, RTL, netlists, graphs, images) to bridge stages of the EDA flow.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

contexts.context: Jansen (From Words to Wires) demonstrates that contemporary large language models can translate high-level natural language device descriptions into concrete electronic designs. The paper frames this skill as analogous to code generation and introduces benchmarks and case studies showing practical results. LLMs can produce parts lists, electronic schematics, and embedded microcontroller code (Arduino ecosystem), and were used as a design assistant for moderately complex devices (e.g., a radiation-powered random number generator, emoji keyboard, visible spectrometer). Evaluations report strong performance on the introduced benchmarks, with models such as GPT-4 and Claude-V1 achieving 60–96% PASS@1 for generating full devices. The authors describe a staged design pipeline (ideation → electronic design including parts lists, schematics, and microcontroller code → physical implementation) and note that generated designs are reviewed and corrected by domain experts before rapid prototyping. The excerpt focuses on natural-language-to-schematic/code capabilities and microcontroller firmware generation; it does not explicitly discuss HDL (FPGA/ASIC) code generation in detail.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

contexts.context: The paper reports that generative AI methods — including GNNs, LLMs, and VAEs — have been applied to analog circuit sizing and related EDA tasks. LLMs in particular have been explored for layout automation, topology generation, optimization, and decision-making; an example cited used an LLM to design ring oscillators and multi-stage amplifiers. Combining generative models (e.g., LLMs) with GNNs has shown promising results for end-to-end tasks such as designing op-amps from specification through layout. LLMs can be adapted to design tasks via supervised fine-tuning, zero- and few-shot learning, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), enabling them to tackle large-scale and cross-domain problems. Nonetheless, the current literature is largely limited to relatively simple circuits with few parameters, and the survey highlights broader IC challenges (data scarcity, PVT variations, layout parasitics, and high-dimensional design spaces) that constrain full automation. Overall, the excerpt indicates LLMs and other generative models can support schematic/topology generation, layout-aware sizing, and natural-language-driven design workflows but remain early-stage for complex analog ICs.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

LLMs have recently received attention for their applications in layout automation [25], [26], [27] and topology generation [28].
Their use extends to optimization tasks [29], [30] and decision-making in circuit design, as demonstrated in [31], where an LLM was used to design ring oscillators and multi-stage amplifiers.

Traditionally used for natural language processing, LLMs have proven adaptable to large-scale design problems and cross-domain tasks via techniques such as supervised fine-tuning, zero- and few-shot learning, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG).
However, most of the research in the analog sizing domain has been limited to simple circuits with a small number of parameters.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents current capabilities and limitations of generative AI (LLMs) and complementary ML models (notably GNNs) for EDA. LLM agents can generate schematic-level designs and code (examples produce PySpice code): AnalogCoder is a training-free LLM that automates schematic tasks and rapidly produces initial designs (20/24 circuits in experiments). Prompt-steering tools like SPICEPilot and few-shot/iterative prompt methods (LADAC) help with parasitic-aware sizing. More advanced pipelines combine LLM in-context learning with optimizers (ADO-LLM integrates Bayesian Optimization) or multi-agent LLM systems (AmpAgent) to automate literature analysis, mathematical reasoning, device sizing, and multi-stage amplifier design, yielding reduced iterations and faster execution. GNNs model circuit netlists as graphs for topology exploration, parasitic prediction, and layout-aware optimization; methods like BagNet, ParaGraph, and MLParest use ML/GNNs to reduce costly simulations or predict layout-dependent parasitics. Reported benefits include faster initial design, reduced simulation iterations, and layout improvements (e.g., cell-area reductions). Key limitations: LLMs struggle with precise numerical/math tasks, often need generated code and simulations for verification, and many approaches lack robust topology-awareness or post-layout/parasitic and PVT handling for unseen topologies.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

contexts.context: The excerpt summarizes how LLMs and generative AI are being applied across EDA tasks. Key capabilities reported include generating HDL/RTL (and syntheses) from prompts or by fine-tuning on domain datasets, fixing erroneous Verilog (e.g., RTLFixer), summarizing and analyzing bugs (ChipNeMo with NVBugs), and producing formal assertions (SVA) for verification (AutoSVA, Orenes-Vera et al.). Evaluation focuses on syntax, functionality and design-quality goals, with metrics like pass@k to measure correctness under multiple sampled outputs. LLMs are also used for security/property extraction from documentation (NSPG) and end-to-end CWE identification and assertion conversion (DIVAS). Workflows often combine Retrieval-Augmented Generation and iterative feedback (compiler error logs, PPA estimates). Domain datasets are created from public RTL, documentation, internal logs, or synthesized by general LLMs; models used range from GPT-3.5/GPT-4 and CodeGen to LLaMA2, sometimes fine-tuned or instruction-tuned. The paper also highlights potential use of LLMs in optimization/logic-synthesis decision sequences (generating optimization steps and arguments) and in broader physical-design stages.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

Orenes-Vera et al. [2023] proposes an iterative methodology based on formal property verification to generate SystemVerilog Assertions (SVA) from a given RTL module.

DIVAS [Paria et al., 2023] proposes a LLM-based end-to-end framework for identify the CWEs for a given System-on-Chip (SoC) specification and employs a novel LLM-based technique to determine the relevant CWEs, which are finally converted into SystemVerilog Assertions using LLMs for verification.
ChipNeMo [Liu et al., 2023a] constructs a domain-specific SFT dataset based on NVIDIA’s internal bug database, NVBugs.
Some works have evolved in constructing corresponding datasets through collecting existed data, including public design source codes, documentation, internal error logs, and bug summaries.

For domain specific data, they either collect existed RTL codes [Liu et al., 2023a, Fu et al., 2023, Thakur et al., 2023b, Dehaerne et al., 2023, Meng et al., 2023], or generate synthesis designs from pre-trained general LLMs [Liu et al., 2023c,b].

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys how LLMs and generative AI can be applied to Electronic Design Automation (EDA). It notes that LLMs show capabilities in context understanding, logical reasoning, and answer generation, and highlights that many EDA artifacts—HDL, specs, logs, and intermediate outputs—are textual, making them amenable to LLM processing. The paper categorizes current LLM4EDA work into three main directions: assistant chatbots (interactive Q&A and knowledge retrieval), HDL and script generation, and HDL verification and analysis. It situates LLMs within an existing trend where deep learning is already applied across chip-design stages (logic synthesis, floorplanning, placement, clock-tree synthesis, routing, and PPA prediction) and emphasizes the availability of cross-stage datasets to train models. The authors also identify future research directions: applying LLMs to logic synthesis and physical design, and exploring multi-modal feature extraction and circuit alignment. Overall, the excerpt supports the view that LLMs can assist engineers via natural-language interaction, generate HDL/code, and be extended toward more automated schematic/physical-design tasks when integrated with domain datasets and workflows.
contexts.question: What are the capabilities of LLMs and generative AI for electronic design automation, including schematic generation, HDL code generation, and circuit design from natural language descriptions?

Since circuits can be depicted in programming language, Hardware Description Language (HDL), specifications and many intermediate outputs through EDA flow are also represented in text format.

In this paper, we present a systematic study on the application of LLMs in the EDA field, categorizing it into the following cases: 1) assistant chatbot, 2) HDL and script generation, and 3) HDL verification and analysis.
Assistant Chatbot. Users can interact with LLMs for knowledge acquisition and Q&A, without spending time in waiting or actively searching for answers.

Additionally, we highlight the future research direction, focusing on applying LLMs in logic synthesis, physical design, multi-modal feature extraction and alignment of circuits.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes how LLMs and generative AI can produce structured text (programming code, Verilog, JSON, XML) and be applied to electronic design automation (EDA). Models can act as interactive design assistants or as autonomous agents that reason and generate designs. Generated HDL (Verilog is cited as suitable for platform schematics) and other code can be executed in tools to produce models and drive downstream actions. Few-shot methods and domain-specific examples/BKMs improve results; reinforcement learning and command-based generation are used commercially. AI-driven generation can create component-level schematics from libraries, assist in component selection and wiring, and automate parts of synthesis, simulation, and formal verification. Benefits include reduced design time, improved system performance, and optimized circuit parameters. However, consistency, domain-specific constraints, verification, and the need for design/build/test cycles remain important challenges; AI outputs require review, validation, and integration into layout and electrical analysis workflows. The paper also suggests combining few-shot/domain tuning with agentic multi-step workflows for generation, critique, and regeneration to improve reliability.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes how generative AI and LLMs are applied to hardware design automation: LLMs can produce component-functionality level Verilog (HDL) that represents higher-level designs which are then mapped to vendor parts according to BOM preferences, enabling optimization for cost, size, part tags, guidelines, customer preferences, or carbon footprint. Schematics can be generated from circuit topology, but placement must mimic manual engineer practices with emphasis on visual clarity; accurate component dimensions and pin locations are required for correct placement and wiring, and multi-page schematics use offpage connectors. Generated designs are editable and can be exported to board layout tools. The text notes that LLM behavior is controlled by parameters like temperature (affecting diversity vs precision) and that iterative prompting/regeneration (pointing out errors) reduces generation errors. It also warns that LLMs may respond inconsistently across multi-stage tasks. A modular mapping approach can enable reuse of functionality across platforms by selecting appropriate vendor components.

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys machine learning applications across the electronic design automation (EDA) flow and outlines capabilities that are relevant to generative and large-model approaches. It emphasizes ML's role in extracting reusable high-level features, replacing empirical choices, and accelerating NP-hard tasks by learning from prior designs. The surveyed work is categorized into decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design—progressing toward greater automation. Deep learning and reinforcement learning have enabled studies that fully automate complex, large design-space tasks by learning predictors and policies online. ML applications span HLS, logic synthesis, placement, routing, testing, verification, and manufacturing. Notably, high-level synthesis converts C/C++/SystemC specifications to HDL but is time-consuming for large systems, so ML-guided design-space exploration (DSE) is crucial. Overall, the paper positions ML as a promising means to reduce manual effort, predict quality without expensive synthesis, guide exploration, and ultimately enable more automated EDA processes.

snippets: Machine learning (ML) is taking an important role in our lives these days, which has been widely used in many scenarios.
ML algorithms focus on extracting high-level features or patterns that can be reused in other related or similar situations, avoiding repeated complicated analysis.

We observe that most work collected in this survey can be grouped into four types: decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design, ordered by decreasing manual efforts and expert experiences in the design procedure, or an increasing degree of automation.

Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL), especially Reinforcement Learning (RL) techniques have stimulated several studies that fully automate some complex design tasks with extremely large design space, where predictors and policies are learned, performed, and adjusted in an online form, showing a promising future of Artificial Intelligence (AI)-assisted automated design.

In recent years, ML for EDA is becoming one of the trending topics, and a lot of studies that use ML to improve EDA methods have been proposed, which cover almost all the stages in the chip design flow, including design space reduction and exploration, logic synthesis, placement, routing, testing, verification, manufacturing, etc.
High-level synthesis (HLS) provides automatic conversion from C/C++/SystemC-based specifications to hardware description languages (HDL).
However, when facing a large-scale system, HLS often takes a long time to finish the synthesis. Consequently, efficient design space exploration (DSE) strategy is crucial in HLS

contexts.context: The excerpt describes how HiePlace was evaluated and compared with existing methods, including Quilter. The authors tested 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic) and implemented HiePlace in C++ with an MP-based model solved by Gurobi. For alignment-aware fine-tuning they use a grid-based local alignment metric (misalignment count, MC), keep first-priority components fixed, and apply backtracking if overlaps occur; the alignment loop uses a 300 s timeout. Quilter is identified as an open-source, RL-based tool that supports pre-placement constraint settings but has high runtime and is more suitable for smaller academic benchmarks (fewer than ~100 components). Consequently, the authors specifically compared HiePlace against Quilter on the academic benchmarks where Quilter’s constraint support and scale make it applicable. The excerpt does not provide numeric benchmark results or quantitative comparisons (e.g., wirelength, runtime, alignment metrics) between HiePlace and Quilter; it only states the scope and rationale for the comparison and some experimental settings.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes an evaluation framework and a benchmark suite of 11 PCB designs for automated placement and routing. Key objective metrics defined for comparison are: design-rule violations (DRVs) including pad/trace/via clearances, through-hole via count, laser via count, and laser via layer count; LvS (logical vs. schematic) errors where a net is unconnected after routing; CPU time; and total trace length. The authors note manufacturing/cost implications (e.g., each laser-via layer pair increases cost by ~40%) and per-net minimum via sizes are enforced. Their router prioritizes LvS connectivity over DRV cleanliness and “never produces LvS errors.” The benchmarks were chosen to span complexity while avoiding many advanced constraints (trace-length matching, differential pairs); full manual placements are provided as known-good references. Table 4.1 lists design characteristics (#components, #nets, #pins, #locked components, design area) for the 11 designs. A repository with designs and more details is provided. The work also mentions ongoing extensions for advanced rules and routing behavior adjustments (e.g., penalizing bends, 45° routing cost).

snippets: We identified design-rule violations (DRVs), through-hole via count, laser via count, and laser via layer count as the key metrics
Metrics are standards used to objectively evaluate layout quality and quantitative compari- son between systems without relying on aesthetics.
An LvS error occurs when a connection that appears in a design’s netlist does not have a corresponding electrical connection after routing.
Our router prioritizes LvS connectivity over DRV cleanness and never produces LvS errors
Through-hole via count equals the number of through-hole vias used in a layout.
Laser via layer count equals the number of adjacent layer pairs connected by laser vias.
Each pair of layers connected by laser vias increases the manufacturing cost by approxi- mately 40% due to process cost and yield losses.
CPU time is the total run time of a layout program.
Total trace length is the combined linear length of all traces in a routed design.
We present a collection of 11 PCB designs to serve as benchmarks for automated layout

These designs were selected because they contain a variety of complexity levels, are small enough to be completed in a reasonable time using our automated framework, and are simple enough that they do not contain many advanced constraints

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that applying state-of-the-art analytical IC placers (e.g., RePlace) to PCB placement failed to converge to good solutions due to issues such as inability to find rotations, dependence on design-dependent smoothing parameters, and lack of support for non-rectilinear geometries. It highlights fundamental PCB vs. VLSI differences: far fewer components on PCBs, complex polygonal (often non-rectilinear and non-convex) component shapes, two-sided/3D placement (mirroring when placed on bottom), and common free-angle/45° rotations. Because of these differences the authors implemented a bespoke placer using stochastic combinatorial optimization: simulated annealing to jointly optimize wirelength, overlap/density, and routability — an approach suitable for the modest-scale component counts typical of PCBs. They provide an open-source, fully automated framework exposing a shared database, placer, autorouter, and DRC; the flow can use their SA placer, KiCad’s autoplacer, or other external tools, and supports KiCad .kicad_pcb import/export and Gerber generation.

snippets: As an initial experiment, we applied the state of the art analytical IC placers [LCC+15, CKKW18] to the PCB placement problem and identified several issues that prevented convergence to good solutions.
The vanilla RePlace implementation fails to successfully find good rotations for individual components.
RePlace is natively incapable of dealing with non-rectilinear geometries.
The number of components in a PCB placement problem range from dozens in small designs to thousands in very large designs, whereas VLSI designs may contain billions of components.
PCB components may have complex polygonal shapes, as opposed to the rectilinear cells of IC design.
PCB components may be placed on either the top of the bottom of the PCB.
PCB components may be freely rotated during placement, and 45-degree rotations are not uncommon in PCB layouts.
Our placer uses stochastic combinatorial optimization which, although typically incapable of placing hundreds of thousands of cells, we successfully apply it to PCB designs.
In this work, we apply simulated annealing[KGV83] to jointly optimize wirelength, overlap/density, and routability.
Our framework is unique in being both open-source and fully-automated.
We are releasing the following modules as part of our framework: 1. shared design database 2. placer 3. autorouter 4. design rule checker (DRC)

Our framework may use any combination of the following: 1. Our placer: We use a simulated annealing (SA) algorithm ... 2. KiCad autoplacer: KiCad has a utility for spreading components on the PCB. KiCad’s auto-placer attempts to minimize air-wire length... 3. Other external tools:

contexts.context: This excerpt documents components of an automated PCB layout framework that are relevant when comparing placement/routing benchmark results (such as HiePlace or Quilter). The placer is a simulated-annealing (SA) approach with a mixed objective that penalizes HPWL, a routing congestion metric, and component overlap. The annealing schedule initially weights HPWL and congestion fully and gradually reweights to prioritize overlap, ending with overlap at 100%. Move sizes are reduced as overlap weight increases, and the final allowed Manhattan move radius is one millimeter. Rotation moves are enabled and fixed at a 25% ratio. The authors tested multiple overlap-penalty formulas and report a three-term measure as their best. For routing, the framework uses a grid-based A* search with cost-based rip-up and reroute; reported experiments used 20 iterations. The excerpt also notes practical differences among tools: KiCad’s autoplacer minimizes air-wire length and biases placement toward one corner and requires the GUI, while FreeRouting produces only DRV-clean (design-rule–clean) topological routes and assumes interactive use. These algorithmic choices (objective composition and weighting schedule, move/rotation parameters, overlap penalty, routing strategy and iterations, and DRV handling) are key factors to consider when interpreting or comparing benchmark results between HiePlace, Quilter, and other automated PCB tools.

snippets: KiCad’s auto-placer attempts to minimize air-wire length, with a preference for compacting components into the lower-right corner of the board.
Our placer uses a simulated annealing strategy with a mixed objective function.
Our objective function penalizes the sum of half-perimeter wire length (HPWL) over all nets, a routing congestion metric[SJ07], and a component overlap score.

At the beginning of annealing, the objective function places 100% of weight on HPWL and congestion and none on overlap. As annealing progresses, the objective function is reweighted, placing more weight on overlap, proportional to the remaining annealing iterations. Annealing ends with overlap weighted at 100%.
At the end of annealing, the allowed component move radius (in Manhattan space) is one millimeter.
We have experimented with several formulas for penalizing overlap. Our best results have been achieved using the following three-term measure:
We fix the ratio of rotation moves at 25%.
Our router uses an iterative cost-based rip-up and reroute strategy based on A* search.
The results for our router, presented in Section 4.10, were routed with 20 iterations.
FreeRouting only performs DRV-clean routing, and will not output routes that violate the design rules.

contexts.context: The paper (IEEE Trans. CAD, DOI:10.1109/TCAD.2025.3580511) presents HiePlace, an MP-based hierarchical placement framework tailored to PCB-specific constraints (irregular outlines, priorities, alignment). To make direct mathematical-programming approaches practical, the authors introduce two acceleration techniques: a dynamic-prioritization stage for core components (reducing solution space) and a relaxation algorithm to minimize the number of boolean variables and further narrow the search. Experimental claims report substantial improvements: 7.7× speedup and 66% cost reduction. The manuscript includes qualitative comparisons (Fig. 2) showing placements by different algorithms and HiePlace variants (e.g., considering component priority and alignment). The paper contrasts HiePlace with prior PCB/VLSI approaches: tree-based (B*tree) and simulated annealing methods emphasize wirelength/area but suffer from incomplete solution coverage, while analytical methods use mathematical/physical models but may not handle PCB irregularities and custom constraints. The excerpt does not mention Quilter or provide head-to-head benchmark numbers versus other automated PCB tools named Quilter, so direct Quilter comparisons are not available in these pages.

contexts.context: The excerpt documents a benchmark suite of PCB designs (bm1–bm11) with varied challenges (narrow aspect ratios, through-hole-only boards, high pin-count modems, unusual footprints) and describes preprocessing to regularize human designs into KiCad format (conversion, removal of non-functional elements, removal of copper pours and manual routing for affected nets, replacing silk-screen with courtyard polygons, simplifying board outlines, locking connectors, adding net classes, and fixing DRC errors). Comparative results use three metrics (via count, total trace length, CPU time) across five placer/router combinations: manual/manual, manual/FreeRouting, our placer/FreeRouting, manual/our router, and fully automatic. Key observations: via counts are presented per configuration and depend strongly on router behavior and a layer-change-cost parameter for the A* router; FreeRouting tends to detour on the same layer while the authors’ router uses more vias; manual placements usually yield shorter trace length than automatic placements; certain designs (bm6 narrow boards, bm8 through-hole layout, bm11 high pin-count tight areas) are particularly challenging for automated placement/routing. The authors also modified FreeRouting for batch use and used KiCad as a recommended open benchmark format.

1. Designs not originally in the KiCad layout format were converted to that format;
3. Copper pour areas (also known as copper fill or zones) were removed, and nets that relied on copper pour for connectivity were manually routed;

5. Silk-screen outlines and designator text were replaced with polygons in the front and back courtyard layers (CrtYd.F and CrtYd.B). These courtyard polygon serve as component outlines for placement purposes;
We propose the KiCad PCB layout format (.kicad pcb) as a preferred standard format for PCB layout benchmarks

We present comparative PCB layout results for each benchmark design in Tables 4.2 (via counts), 4.3 (total trace length), and 4.4 (CPU time) using the metrics for five different combinations of placers and routers:
1. manual placement and routing,
2. manual placement with FreeRouting (FR) autorouter,
3. our placer with FreeRouting autorouter,
4. manual placement with our autorouter, and
5. a fully automatic layout using our framework.
Via counts for each placer and router combination are presented in Table 4.2.
As our router uses A* search, via count is heavily dependent on a “layer change cost” parameter that penalizes expanding search to other layers while routing.
Manual layouts generally result in less total trace length compared to automatic placement.
FreeRouting prefers to take more detours on the same layer, while our router prefers vias.
We modiﬁed the basic implementation of FreeRouting to allow FreeRouting to be run in “batch mode” without a GUI.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports HiePlace benchmark and evaluation details: experiments were run on the first 10 PCB designs with feasible placements and show that HiePlace "effectively minimizes net crossings and reduces wire-length." Compared to the baseline (and in context compared with Floorplet), HiePlace "achieves an average reduction of 16% in wirelength and 46% in misaligned regions while effectively minimizing the impact of line crossings." The authors highlight "significant improvements in both runtime and wirelength reduction compared to existing methods." HiePlace uses two acceleration techniques: dynamic programming (DP) for first-priority component placement and a relaxation algorithm that "minimizes Boolean variables while effectively reducing the solution space." The paper notes limitations and future work: routability evaluation is currently a superposition of indicators like net intersection and alignment and needs a comprehensive model, and double-sided layouts are not yet supported but could be modeled as two interconnected planes. The excerpt does not mention Quilter specifically; comparisons are made to baseline methods and Floorplet.

snippets: We compared the solution on the first 10 PCB designs, which have feasible placement results.
The results indicate that our method effectively minimizes net crossings and reduces wire- length.
our approach achieves an average reduction of 16% in wirelength and 46% in misaligned regions while effectively minimizing the impact of line crossings.
Experimental results demonstrated signiﬁcant improvements in both runtime and wirelength reduction compared to existing methods.

Our framework introduced two innovative acceleration techniques: DP for first-priority component placement and a relaxation algorithm that minimizes Boolean variables while effectively reducing the solution space.
Regarding routability evaluation, we currently view it as a superposition of different indicators, such as net intersection and alignment.
For double-sided layouts, our framework could be extended to treat the PCB as two interconnected planes, expanding the state space to include component placement on either the front or back sides.
In this article, we presented HiePlace, an efﬁcient MP-based placement framework tailored for PCBs.

contexts.context: The paper implements HiePlace in C++ and evaluates it on 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industry, 4 academic). HiePlace is compared against prior MP-based methods and the open-source Quilter tool (evaluated on academic benchmarks bm1–bm4). HiePlace consistently produces better placement quality: shorter wirelength, lower cost, improved alignment and routability. Quantitatively, HiePlace cuts cost substantially—on average reducing cost by 66% and 18% versus two baselines—and requires only about 13% of their runtime. Against Quilter, HiePlace achieves dramatic improvements: cost reductions of 76.7%–91.3% across academic benchmarks, an example improvement from 19.46×10^3 to 3.58×10^3 (81.6%), and runtime drops from Quilter’s 34–75 minutes to just a few seconds (runtime reductions reported up to ~99.4%–99.9%). The MP-based prior solver can achieve shorter wirelength in some cases but fails to scale/produce feasible solutions on larger benchmarks within time limits; HiePlace balances scalability, speed, and solution quality for large, dense industrial PCB instances.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes two benchmarks and evaluation setup used in From Words to Wires. PINS100 is a benchmark of 100 common electronic components (components range from 2 to 40 pins) used to evaluate models' knowledge of component pinouts. Evaluation uses two binary PASS@1-like metrics: a strict measure (all pins must be correct) and a permissive measure (only function-critical pins required). Models evaluated include instruction-tuned OpenAI CHATGPT (GPT-3.5-TURBO), GPT-4, and Anthropic CLAUDE (versions referenced). Reported PINS100 results: GPT-4 achieves the highest strict scoring performance, generating accurate pinouts for 74% of components; GPT-3.5 and CLAUDE-V1 achieve ~55% and 56% strict accuracy respectively. Under permissive scoring GPT-4 produces pinouts that include the most critical pins for 86% of components. MICRO25 is a 25-task benchmark aimed at Arduino microcontroller-driven device creation spanning input, protocols, output, sensors, and logic; models were prompted to output device specs (BOM, pinouts, schematics, code) in annotated JSON. The excerpt does not provide MICRO25 quantitative performance numbers for GPT-4.

snippets: Benchmark: We assembled a benchmark of electronic component pinouts, PINS100, containing 100 common parts frequently used in circuits
Components range from 2 to 40 pins
Evaluation: We evaluate using two binary measures of accuracy analogous to the PASS@1 code-generation metric (Kulal et al., 2019).
Models: We evaluate on instruction-tuned models including OpenAI’s CHATGPT (GPT-3.5-TURBO) and GPT-4 (OpenAI, 2023), and Anthropic CLAUDE-V14.

GPT-4 achieves the highest strict scoring performance, generating accurate pinouts for 74% of components, while both GPT3.5 and CLAUDE-V1 achieve similar levels of performance, generating correct pinouts for 55% and 56% of components, respectively.
Permissive scoring increases performance, with the best-scoring GPT-4 model capable of generating pinouts that include the most critical pins for 86% of electrical components in the benchmark.

Benchmark: To assess a model’s ability to create microcontroller-driven electronic devices, we developed a benchmark, MICRO25, that includes 25 tasks intended for the common ARDUINO microcontroller ecosystem.
These tasks, shown in Table 2, span 5 core categories including: input, interface protocols, output, sensors, and logic.
Representations: Models were given formatation prompts to export all generated elements (bill of materials, pinouts, schematics, and code) in an annotated JSON format

contexts.context: The paper (From Words to Wires) presents a workflow (WORDS2WIRES) that generates full device specifications from natural language: bill of materials, component pinouts, schematics (as netlists), and microcontroller code. It evaluates pinout generation accuracy on a 100-component benchmark (Pins100, reported in Table 1). According to Table 1, GPT-4 achieves 74% under strict (exact) pinout scoring and 86% under permissive scoring; for context, GPT-3.5 scored 55% strict / 74% permissive and Claude scored 56% strict / 72% permissive. The paper also uses a MICRO25 benchmark to assess single-shot generation of error-free devices (single-turn, strict binary success similar to PASS@1), though the excerpt does not provide numeric MICRO25 results for GPT-4. An example device in the paper was generated by GPT-4. The authors note that generating structured device specifications before code (and using larger model context windows) enables producing thousands-of-token specifications and helps condition downstream code and schematic generation.

contexts.context: The excerpt reports that GPT-4 attains high generation accuracy in constrained settings (Experiment 2), and can produce simple electronic device designs much faster than students—often in minutes versus hours or days. In a more open, complex generation condition (Experiment 3), however, nearly all generated devices contained errors and required domain-expert correction. Of six open-generation devices, three (emoji keyboard, non-contact temperature sensor, ultrasonic glasses) were produced in essentially functional forms before adding extra requirements. Generated devices in this work are small in scope (typically fewer than 50 components, under 50 lines of code, and run on Arduino microcontrollers with ~2K memory). The authors note that successfully designing all devices in the MICRO25 benchmark would equal the performance of a strong undergraduate microcontroller-design student, implying current LLMs are not yet at that full benchmark capability. Additional limitations include manual physical construction, lack of simulation tools for automated evaluation, safety concerns, and the need for domain expert review for real-world or safety-critical use. The excerpt does not provide numeric results for Pins100 or full MICRO25 pass rates.

contexts.context: This paper (ChipGPT) addresses limitations of raw LLM-generated HDL (Verilog RTL) by inserting a post-LLM design-space-exploration (DSE) stage to optimize hardware metrics (power, performance, area — PPA). The workflow: generate prompts to an LLM to produce initial Verilog programs; use an output manager to correct and optimize those programs; collect variants into a final design space implemented as an HDL search tree; run an enumeration/search over that space to select designs meeting target PPA metrics. The approach explicitly avoids modifying LLM weights and integrates with LLM APIs. The authors claim improved Verilog quality and broader optimization capability compared to native LLM outputs and prior agile chip-design methods; experimental results reportedly show improved effectiveness. The excerpt does not include benchmark names like Pins100 or Micro25 nor any GPT-4 numeric performance results on circuit design, so those specific metrics/results are not available here.

snippets: Improving Large Language Model Hardware Generating Quality through Post-LLM Search
existing LLM-based hardware generating frameworks generate verilog register transfer language(RTL) without considering its performance, power, area(PPA).
we design a post LLM search approach to merge design space exploration(DSE) process into current LLM hardware generation workflow, which enables the PPA optimization.
our framework begins by generating prompts for the LLM, which then produces initial Verilog programs.
an output manager corrects and optimizes these programs before collecting them into the final design space, which is constructed as a HDL search tree.
Eventually, our work will do search through this space to select the optimal design under the target metrics.
Our framework, ChipGPT, integrates the design exploration process into LLM-based hardware generation
Proposing a hardware description language search tree to generate Verilog programs without modifying LLM weights, seamlessly integrating with the latest LLM APIs.
Overcoming challenges of power/area/performance agnostic LLMs in chip design through a post-LLM search method with an efficient output manager.
ChipGPT outperforms previous agile chip logic design methods and native ChatGPT, showing improvement in programmability and scalability, with potential for larger-scale chip design extension.
Experimental results show improved effectiveness.

contexts.context: The paper introduces two evaluation suites for language-model-driven circuit design: PINS100 (assessing model knowledge of electrical components) and MICRO25 (evaluating a model’s capability to design common microcontroller circuits and Arduino ecosystem code involving I/O, sensors, motors, protocols, and logic). The authors frame the task as converting high-level textual descriptions into complete device designs, encompassing ideation, parts lists, electronic schematics, and microcontroller code, then moving to physical implementation. In their reported results, large models "such as GPT-4 and Claude-V1" achieved between 60% and 96% PASS@1 on generating full devices across these benchmarks. The work also presents six case studies (e.g., a radiation-powered random number generator, emoji keyboard, visible spectrometer) showing language models used as design assistants, and notes evaluation and practical implementation steps using rapid prototyping.

snippets: We introduce two benchmarks:2023 trical components, and MICRO25, evaluating a model’s capability to design common micro-Oct controller circuits and code in the ARDUINO ecosystem that involve input, output, sensors,13 motors, protocols, and logic – with models such as GPT-4 and Claude-V1 achieving between 60% to 96% PASS@1 on generating full de-vices.
PINS100, assessing model knowledge of elec-

language models have a previously unknown skill – the capacity to gen-erate working electronic devices from high-level textual descriptions – effectively bridging the gap between the words of a device description to the wires of a device design.

These steps include: ideation, electronic design (including gen-erating parts lists, electronic schematics, and code for embedded processors called microcontrollers), followed ultimately by physical implementation

We include six case studies of using language models as a design assistant for mod-erately complex devices, such as a radiation-powered random number generator, an emoji keyboard, a visible spectrometer, and several assistive devices

contexts.context: The paper benchmarks language-model-based circuit/design generation on two tasks: PINS100 and MICRO25. On PINS100 the models show "moderate-to-high" proficiency at component-level knowledge. GPT-4 outperforms Claude-V1 on MICRO25, producing "25 fully-functional devices" and achieving near-perfect performance on that benchmark. In a more open, qualitative design setting (Experiment 3) GPT-4 can produce designs that nearly meet specifications for six complex devices, but these outputs typically require "moderate correction by domain experts" and manual fixes before physical construction. Major challenges include prompt sensitivity, the models' tendency to rely on obsolete or end-of-life electronic parts, use deprecated libraries, and poor generation of low-level device drivers (often hallucinating libraries or producing first-pass implementations that need extensive fixes). Manual evaluation (similar to Copilot-style workflows) is currently necessary because "the lack of electronic simulators with large libraries of simulated devices" blocks automated end-to-end testing. The authors note that iterative generation from core requirements to finer details can improve performance, and that task-specific fine-tuning or better simulators could address robustness issues.

contexts.context: The excerpt (references list) shows that machine learning techniques have been applied across many EDA tasks relevant to placement, routing, logic synthesis, and physical design. Examples include deep learning for logic optimization and timing signoff, learning-based floorplanning and local-search heuristics, prediction of IR drop, reinforcement learning for logic synthesis, neural-network migration for mask/ILT co-optimization, and CNN ensembles for layout hotspot detection. There are also works on learning combinatorial optimization over graphs and automated heuristic design for SAT solvers, indicating use of graph neural networks, supervised models, and heuristic/meta-learning. The references include theoretical foundations (e.g., multilayer feedforward networks as universal approximators) and cover applications such as surrogate modeling, prediction (timing, power, IR drop), algorithm-selection/heuristic learning, and replacing expensive checks with learned approximations. While the excerpt does not present the survey’s explicit taxonomy, the cited works imply common taxonomic axes: EDA stage (placement/floorplanning, routing, timing, power, mask/layout, verification), ML paradigm (supervised regression/classification, reinforcement learning, graph methods, ensembles), and use-case (prediction/surrogate, heuristic learning, optimization/auto-tuning).

contexts.context: The excerpt surveys ML applications across HLS, logic synthesis, placement, routing and other physical-design subproblems. For design-space exploration and HLS, supervised models (Lasso, Ridge, ANN, SVM, Random Forest, XGBoost), Gaussian Processes, and Graph Neural Networks are used for timing/resource/latency prediction, cross-platform prediction, and operation-delay estimation. Active learning and GP/RF are applied to reduce sample needs and to protect Pareto-optimal designs (ATNE uses RF to “minimize the risk of losing ‘good’ designs”). ML also augments optimization: STAGE learns promising starting points for simulated annealing; FSA uses decision trees to generate new samples; ML predicts hyper-parameter effects on solution quality and runtime to tune SA/GA/ACO. For logic synthesis, ML schedules traditional optimizers (LSOracle uses DNN), classifies synthesis flows with CNNs, and employs reinforcement learning (GCN, A2C) to guide transformations. Placement work (PADE) extracts/evaluates data-paths with SVM and NN and separates their placement; routing uses CNNs (RouteNet) for DRC/hotspot detection. The authors recommend public DSE benchmarks and customized models combining domain knowledge for further gains.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

snippets: ATNE [112] utilizes Random Forest (RF) to aid the DSE process.
ATNE focuses on estimating and minimizing the risk of losing “good” designs due to learning inaccuracy.
STAGE [74] is proposed for DSE of many-core systems.
The motivating observation of STAGE is that the performance of simulated annealing is highly sensitive to the starting point of the search process.
Fast Simulated Annealing (FSA) [107] utilizes the decision tree to improve the performance of SA.
For each algorithm, the authors build an ML model that predicts the resultant design quality (measured by Average Distance to the Reference Set, ADRS) and runtime from hyper-parameter settings.
Timing and resource usage predic-   Lasso, ANN, XGBoost       [30]
Operation delay                 Graph Neural Network      [143]
Reduce the risk of losing Pareto de-  Random Forest              [112]
Initial point selection               Quadratic regression        [74]
Generation of new sample           Decision Tree               [107]
Hyper-parameter selection          Decision Tree               [149]

In the logic synthesis and physical design stage, there are many key sub-problems that can benefit from the power of ML models, including lithography hotspot detection, path classification, congestion prediction, placement guide, fast timing analysis, logic synthesis scheduling, and so on.
LSOracle [115] relies on DNN to dynamically decide which optimizer should be applied to different parts of the circuit.
The CNN takes the one-hot encoding of synthesis flows as inputs and outputs the possibilities of the input flow belonging to different QoR metric levels.
Reinforcement learning is also employed for logic synthesis in [48, 56].
PADE [150] proposes a placement process with automatic data path extraction and evaluation
PADE is a force-directed global placer, which applies SVM and NN to extract and evaluate the data path patterns
RouteNet [154] is the first work to employ CNN for design rule checking (DRC) hotspot detection.

contexts.context: The excerpt highlights concrete ML applications across EDA stages and provides a functionality-based taxonomy. For power estimation at RTL, methods such as PCA, MLP and CNN are used (PRIMAL), achieving large speedups (PRIMAL: 50×) with modest error (<5%), while GNN-based GRANNITE leverages netlist structure to improve transferability and runtime (two orders of magnitude speedup, ~5.5% error). For combinatorial reasoning and verification, GNNs (e.g., NeuroSAT) and combinations of GNN feature extractors with reinforcement learning have been applied to learn heuristics and guide SAT solvers. Physical design/placement has been accelerated by reusing deep learning frameworks: DREAMPlace treats placement as analogous to NN training and achieves >40× speedups on GPUs. Auto-tuning of design flows uses collaborative filtering, tree-based models (XGBoost), and deep RL with unsupervised GNN features to recommend tool parameters. The authors group ML roles in EDA into four categories: decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design; ML is already used to select toolchains for logic synthesis and other synthesis tasks.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

snippets: PRIMAL [175] is an RTL power estimation framework based on several ML methods, including Principal Component Analysis (PCA), MLP and CNN.
PRIMAL can achieve a 50× speedup than gate-level power estimation flow with an average error below 5%.
GRANNITE shows good transferability among different designs by utilizing more hardware details.
Compared to a traditional probabilistic switching activity estimation, GRANNITE achieves a speed up of two orders of magnitude on average, and the average relative error is within 5.5%.
Machine Learning for SAT Solver
Selsam et al. [133] propose to train a GNN (called NeuroSAT) to classify SAT problems as satisfiable or unsatisfiable.
In recent studies, a common practice is to use GNN for feature extraction and reinforcement learning for learning the policy.
Lin et al. [93] leverage the mature deep learning engines to build a GPU-accelerated placement framework called DREAMPlace
The key insight of this paper is that the analytical placement problem is analogous to the training of a NN model.
DREAMPlace demonstrates over 40× speedup against CPU-based multi-threaded tools [26, 101].
Auto-tuning design flow
Kwon et al. [78] propose the first recommender system based on the collaborative filtering algorithm.
A recent study [153] also employs a tree-based XGBoost model for efficient tuning.
In [4], a deep RL framework that adopts unsupervised GNN to generate features is developed to automatically tune the placement tool parameters.

Based on the functionality of ML in the EDA workflow, we can group most researches into four categories: decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design.
ML has been used to select among available tool-chains for logic synthesis [115, 167] , mask synthesis [163], and topology selection in analog

contexts.context: The excerpt summarizes how machine learning augments many stages of the EDA flow. It notes that logic synthesis relies on heuristics and that many ML methods are applied there. Placement and routing are strongly coupled, motivating "routing-aware" ML methods in physical design. Fabrication steps such as mask optimization and lithography simulation are challenging and have seen recent ML approaches. Verification and manufacturing testing also use ML for test-set optimization and complexity reduction. The survey frames ML taxonomically by learning paradigms (supervised, unsupervised, active, reinforcement) and by model families: classical ML (linear regression, RF, SVM, XGBoost) and deep models (CNN, RNN, GAN, DRL, GNN). It maps model strengths to data types (CNN for grid/2-D imagery, RNN for sequences, GNN for graphs) and highlights DRL for large decision spaces. For high-level synthesis (HLS), ML is used for fast/accurate result estimation, to refine design-space exploration algorithms, and to recast DSE as active learning. Overall, ML is presented as a data-driven supplement to analytical models that reduces cost/time and addresses non-optimal heuristic processes across placement, routing, synthesis, fabrication, verification, and testing.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

snippets: Logic synthesis is a complicated process that usually cannot be solved optimally, and hence the heuristic algorithms are widely used in this stage, which include lots of ML methods [48, 56, 115, 167].
The placement and routing are strongly coupled.
many ML-based routing-aware methods are proposed to improve the performance of physical design [6, 27, 89, 106, 150, 154].
Mask optimization and lithography simulation are still challenging problems. Recently, various ML-based methods are applied in the lithography simulation and mask synthesis [20, 43, 159, 163, 165].

Traditional random or automated test set generation methods are far away from optimal, therefore, there exist many studies that apply ML methods to optimize test set generation for verification [24, 33, 38, 47, 49, 57, 69, 135, 145, 146].
studies have focused on applying ML techniques for test set optimization [100, 120, 140, 141] and test complexity reduction [5, 34, 142].
Machine learning is a class of algorithms that automatically extract information from datasets or prior knowledge.
In general, ML-based solutions can be categorized according to their learning paradigms: supervised learning, unsupervised learning, active learning, and reinforcement learning.

popular models in recent EDA studies include convolutional neural network (CNN) [37, 111], recurrent neural networks (RNN) [83, 148], generative adversarial network (GAN) [165], deep reinforcement learning (DRL) [113, 147] and graph neural networks (GNN) [147, 168].
DRL has achieved great success in complicated tasks with large decision space (e.g., Go game [138]).
GNN is proposed for data organized as graphs.

CNN models are composed of convolutional layers ... While CNN is suitable for feature extraction on grid structure data like 2-D image, RNN is good at processing sequential data such as text or audio.

ML techniques have been applied to improve HLS tools from the following three aspects: fast and accurate result estimation [30, 37, 108, 109, 143, 164, 172], refining conventional DSE algorithms [74, 107, 149], and reforming DSE as an active-learning problem [94, 95, 112, 180].

contexts.context: The excerpt presents a taxonomy of ML functionality in EDA and specific methods applied to placement, routing, logic synthesis and physical design. Four high-level ML usages are described: Decision making for tool-chain and hyper-parameter selection (e.g., using DNNs for logic synthesis choices), Performance prediction (supervised/unsupervised models used for fast QoR estimation such as congestion prediction and hotspot detection), Black-box optimization for design-space exploration (active learning with surrogate models like GP and other predictive models), and Automated design (AI-driven tasks such as placement and analog device sizing). Common algorithms across tasks include Linear Regression, Random Forests, XGBoost, CNNs, SVMs, GANs, and domain-specific choices like RL and GCN for automated placement. The table maps tasks to algorithms and typical inputs/outputs (e.g., placement & routing using SVM/CNN/GAN with netlist or layout features to predict wire-length and congestion; physical implementation using SVM/CNN/XG-Boost for lithography hotspots, IR drop, and delay). Key findings emphasize replacing brute-force/empirical configuration with ML, using surrogate models for guided search, and active learning strategies to sample new design points.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

snippets: Decision making in traditional methods. The configurations of EDA tools, including the choice of algorithm or hyper-parameters, have a strong impact on the efficiency of the procedure and quality of the outcome.
Logic synthesis        DNN             RTL descriptions      Choice of the work- flow and optimizer
Placement and routing    SVM, CNN, GAN, MARS, Random For- est etc.
Physical implementation   SVM,  CNN,  XG-Boost, GAN, etc.
Performance prediction. This type of tasks mainly use supervised or unsupervised learning algorithms.
Typical applications of this type include congestion prediction in placement & routing and hotspot detection in manufacturability estimation (Table 8).
The most commonly-used models are Linear Regression, Random Forests, XGBoost, and prevailing CNNs.
Black-box optimization. This type of tasks mainly use active learning.
Options of the surrogate model include GP, along with all the models used in performance prediction [105, 112].
Automated design. Some studies leverage AI to automate design tasks that rely heavily on human efforts. Typical applications are placement [113] and analog device sizing [134, 147, 148].

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses evaluation goals and metrics for generated RTL (syntax, functionality, and design quality such as PPA), and notes VerilogEval's use of a pass@k metric to quantify correctness of generated RTL. It surveys HDL verification and analysis applications of LLMs: domain-specific SFT datasets for bug summarization (ChipNeMo), automated code repair (RTLFixer) using RAG/ReAct-style feedback, and LLM-based assertion generation and checking to find RTL bugs and security issues. Frameworks like NSPG and DIVAS identify properties/CWEs from documentation and convert them into SystemVerilog Assertions. The paper covers dataset construction for fine-tuning LLMs on EDA artifacts and provides a table of backbones (e.g., RTLCoder, VerilogEval, ChatEDA) and model sizes used in recent works. In outlook, it frames logic synthesis as a sequential decision optimization problem (pre-mapping, technology mapping, post-mapping) and notes existing ML/optimization approaches—heuristics, Bayesian Optimization, and Reinforcement Learning—and proposes that LLMs can generate optimization sequences and arguments given HDL and PPA targets to iteratively improve QoR. Physical design is briefly mentioned but not detailed in this excerpt.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies
