snippets: 2. Functionality goal. It means the functionality of generated RTL should be exactly the same as user’s expectation.
3. Design quality goal. If the generated RTL pass both above-mentioned unit tests, we need to further check its design quality, e.g. PPA.
Based on above-mentioned unit tests, VerilogEval [Liu et al., 2023b] proposes to use pass@k metric to further reflect the correctness of generated RTL codes:
3.3 HDL Verification and Analysis
To assist engineers in bug summarization and analysis, ChipNeMo [Liu et al., 2023a] constructs a domain-specific SFT dataset based on NVIDIA’s internal bug database, NVBugs.
RTLFixer [Tsai et al., 2023] proposes a paradigm for fixing erroneous Verilog codes directly.
LLMs also demonstrate the capability in assertion checking, which is effective at finding intricate RTL bugs and security issues.
NSPG [Meng et al., 2023] proposes a framework to identify essential properties from Intellectual Property (IP) cores documentations.
4  Datasets of LLM4EDA
Table 2: Type and size of corresponding LLMs selected in each method.
RTLCoder [Liu et al., 2023c]
VerilogEval [Liu et al., 2023b]
ChatEDA [He et al., 2023]
6.1 LLM in Logic Synthesis
Logic synthesis transforms a high-level description of a design, e.g. Verilog, into an optimized gate-level representation.

Current works utilize heuristics [Li et al., 2022b], Bayesian Optimization (BO) [Grosnit et al., 2022] Reinforcement Learning (RL) [Hosny et al., 2020, Yuan et al., 2023] to search the well-performed optimization sequence.
With respect to LLM, taking HDL of design and appropriate prompts as input (e.g., containing target PPA), LLM can generate the sequence of optimization along with their respective arguments.

contexts.context: The provided excerpt is a bibliography from the ML-for-EDA survey covering many works across placement, routing, logic/RTL synthesis, physical design and adjacent tasks. It shows ML methods applied to prediction (RouteNet for routability, PowerNet/GridNet for IR-drop and power/EM prediction), optimization and control (GCN-RL for transistor sizing; placement works like PADE and "Keep it Straight"), generative design (WellGAN, LithoGAN, GAN-OPC for layout and mask optimization), verification and testing (neural-network-guided coverage-directed test generation), algorithm/parameter selection (SATzilla portfolio selection; FIST feature-importance sampling for flow parameter tuning), and acceleration (GPU-accelerated simulation, HLS timing prediction). Common model families include graph neural networks, convolutional nets, generative adversarial networks, reinforcement learning, tree-based and portfolio methods. Cross-cutting themes visible in the references are transferability of models across designs, using graph representations for circuits, ML-guided parameter tuning and flow selection, and applying ML both for fast prediction and for guiding optimization/generation in classical EDA flows.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

contexts.context: This excerpt from the ML for EDA survey focuses on using machine learning for high-level synthesis (HLS) result estimation and design-space exploration (DSE). The typical workflow trains models that take HLS reports as inputs and predict post-implementation metrics to avoid expensive full implementation runs. Data processing involves collecting HLS and implementation reports across designs and targets, extracting features (see Table 1 categories such as clock periods, resources, logic/arithmetic ops, memory, multiplexers), and applying feature selection. Regression and ensemble models (linear regression, neural networks, SVM, random forest) are used to predict timing, resource usage, and throughput; one ensemble achieved >95% accuracy. Estimation quality is reported using RAE and relative RMSE. Graph Neural Networks have been customized to predict operation delay by modeling dataflow-graph associations, reducing RMSE by 72% for Vivado HLS. For cross-platform prediction, XPPE uses a neural network taking target FPGA specs, HLS resource usage, and application characteristics to estimate speedup over an ARM core. The excerpt also notes DSE reforms including active learning and improved sampling/search strategies.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

contexts.context: The survey organizes ML for EDA work both by EDA stages (HLS, logic synthesis and physical design — placement and routing, mask synthesis, analog, testing/verification, etc.) and by a taxonomy of four ML roles: decision making in traditional tool flows, performance prediction, black-box optimization for design-space exploration (DSE), and automated design. Decision-making ML replaces empirical choices (tool chains, hyper-parameters) to speed workflows. Performance-prediction models estimate design quality from prior databases to avoid expensive synthesis runs. Black-box optimization uses predictive models plus sampling strategies to guide DSE. Recent deep learning advances, particularly reinforcement learning, enable more fully automated solutions for very large design spaces. The paper emphasizes ML’s potential to capture reusable patterns (avoiding repeated analysis), accelerate solutions for NP-complete EDA problems, and improve scalability. Most surveyed work is recent (within about five years), and the survey reviews methods across the EDA flow while also discussing ML perspectives and future trends.
contexts.question: ML for EDA survey: machine learning methods applied to placement routing logic synthesis physical design, key findings and taxonomies

snippets: We observe that most work collected in this survey can be grouped into four types: decision making in traditional methods, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design.
The opportunity of ML in EDA starts from decision making in traditional methods

ML is also used for performance prediction, where a model is trained from a database of previously implemented designs to predict the quality of new designs, helping engineers to evaluate new designs without the time-consuming synthesis procedure.
black-box optimization, where the entire procedure of design space exploration (DSE) is guided by a predictive ML model and a sampling strategy supported by ML theories.

Recent advances in Deep Learning (DL), especially Reinforcement Learning (RL) techniques have stimulated several studies that fully automate some complex design tasks with extremely large design space

This survey gives a comprehensive review of some recent important studies applying ML to solve some EDA important problems. The review of these studies is organized according to their corresponding stages in the EDA flow.
High-level synthesis (HLS) provides automatic conversion from C/C++/SystemC-based specifications to hardware description languages (HDL).
Logic synthesis converts the behavioral level description to the gate level description, which is one of the most important problems in EDA.

answer.formatted_answer:
Question: Provide a state-of-the-art landscape of generative, AI-assisted, and automated tools for electronic design automation (EDA), schematic capture, and printed circuit board (PCB) design as of 2025. Cover: (a) mainstream schematic/PCB design suites and their automation/AI features (KiCad and its scripting/IPC API, Altium Designer and Altium 365 / Co-Designer AI, Cadence Allegro/OrCAD X and the Allegro AI/Optimality engines, Siemens Xpedition / PADS, Zuken CR-8000, Autodesk Fusion Electronics / EAGLE, DipTrace, EasyEDA); (b) AI-native and generative EDA startups and products (Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, DeepPCB / InstaDeep, Celus, CELUS Design Platform, Diadem, Cofactr, Allspice, Snapmagic/SnapEDA, Spectra/Untether, Microsoft/Google ML-placement efforts); (c) cloud/CI-friendly and code-based hardware description ecosystems (atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA, Edea, Horizon EDA); and (d) for each, the concrete generative/automation capability offered (component selection, schematic generation, auto placement, autorouting, DFM/DRC, BOM and supply-chain optimization) and its limitations (manufacturability, editability, controllability, scriptability for version control / CI). Quote benchmark or case-study results where available. Emphasize which tools are open-source or scriptable and therefore suitable for an open-hardware, CI-driven workflow. Cite vendor pages and ~5-8 recent (2021-2025) peer-reviewed or arXiv papers.

# State-of-the-Art Landscape of Generative, AI-Assisted, and Automated Tools for Electronic Design Automation, Schematic Capture, and PCB Design (2025)

## 1. Introduction

The electronics design automation (EDA) landscape is undergoing a rapid transformation driven by machine learning (ML), large language models (LLMs), and generative AI. A comprehensive 2021 survey catalogued ML applications across the entire EDA hierarchy—from high-level synthesis through placement, routing, verification, and manufacturing—identifying four principal ML roles: decision making, performance prediction, black-box optimization, and automated design (huang2021machinelearningfor pages 3-5, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 1-3). Since then, the field has accelerated with LLM-based code and schematic generation, reinforcement-learning (RL) placement engines, and cloud-native AI-assisted design platforms. This report provides a structured overview of the tool landscape as of 2025, organized into (a) mainstream EDA suites, (b) AI-native startups and products, (c) code-based and CI-friendly ecosystems, and (d) specific generative/automation capabilities and their limitations, with benchmark results where available.

---

## 2. Mainstream Schematic/PCB Design Suites and Their Automation/AI Features

The following table summarizes the major commercial and open-source EDA suites and their current AI/automation capabilities:

| Tool Name | Vendor/Type | License/Open-Source | AI/Automation Features (component selection, schematic generation, auto-placement, autorouting, DFM/DRC, BOM/supply chain) | Scriptability/CI Suitability | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KiCad | KiCad project; desktop schematic/PCB suite | Open-source (GPL) | Strong automation but little built-in AI: Python scripting/plugins, Action Plugins, CLI exporters, IPC/API ecosystem, netlist/BOM generation, ERC/DRC, push-and-shove router, external autorouting via FreeRouting, library-table driven reuse; widely used as open benchmark format and automation backbone in research workflows (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 89-94, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 94-101) | Excellent for open-hardware CI: text-based files, Python automation, BOM/Gerber generation in pipelines, good Git friendliness; strongest fit for reproducible/open workflows | No first-party generative schematic or placement AI; FreeRouting integration is external and limited; complex constraint management and enterprise PLM/supply-chain tooling lag top commercial suites |
| Altium Designer / Altium 365 | Altium; enterprise PCB suite + cloud collaboration | Proprietary/commercial | Mature automation stack: ActiveBOM and supply-chain visibility, rules/constraints, interactive routing, xSignals/high-speed checks, cloud collaboration/review, MCAD co-design; emerging AI/copilot-style assistance in Altium 365/CoDesigner positioning around search, documentation and design assistance rather than fully automatic PCB generation | Moderate: scripts/APIs and cloud workflows exist, but CI is less open than code-first tools; version control better in Altium 365 than classic binary-file workflows | Proprietary stack; editability/version diffs weaker than plain-text/open formats; AI features are assistive and workflow-centric, not fully controllable generative placement/routing |
| Cadence Allegro / OrCAD X | Cadence; high-end PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Very strong automation: constraint-driven design, signal/power integrity integration, Allegro routing engines, topology planning, DFM/DRC, library/data management; AI/ML positioning via Allegro X AI/optimization engines for placement/routing guidance and productivity; strong manufacturing and enterprise flows | Good enterprise automation through Tcl/SKILL/APIs and batch flows, but less Git/CI-native than text/code-based ecosystems | Expensive and complex; strong automation still requires expert constraint setup; closed formats and licensing make open-hardware/CI workflows harder than KiCad/SKiDL-style stacks |
| Siemens Xpedition / PADS Professional | Siemens EDA; enterprise PCB suite | Proprietary/commercial | Constraint-driven layout, advanced routing, HyperLynx SI/PI analysis, DFM/manufacturing links, enterprise library management; strong automation in rule checking, design reuse, and verification; Siemens portfolio also connects to Valor-style manufacturing analysis in broader flow | Enterprise scripting/integration exists, but cloud-native CI friendliness is limited relative to open text-first tools | Powerful but heavyweight; AI is mostly optimization/analysis assist rather than transparent generative design; high barrier for small teams and open-hardware collaboration |
| Zuken CR-8000 | Zuken; enterprise system-level and PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Strong system/board co-design, 3D multi-board, harness/backplane integration, rule-driven placement and routing, advanced constraints, DFM/DRC/manufacturing preparation, parts/library governance; automation is rule-centric more than LLM/generative | Enterprise integrations exist, but not especially Git/CI-native; better suited to controlled corporate flows | Closed ecosystem; steep learning curve; limited visibility/editability for code-review-style hardware workflows; AI-native features less prominent than in newer startup tools |
| Autodesk Fusion Electronics / EAGLE | Autodesk; integrated ECAD/MCAD, cloud-connected | Proprietary/commercial (free tiers historically limited) | Good mainstream automation: schematic capture, push-and-shove routing, autorouter, MCAD integration in Fusion, library/BOM generation, cloud project sharing; useful for mechanical-electrical co-design more than AI-native generation | Some automation/export possible, but weaker scripting and CI ergonomics than KiCad/code-first stacks | EAGLE-to-Fusion transition created ecosystem uncertainty; less capable for advanced constraints than Cadence/Altium; limited built-in AI and weaker open workflow support |
| DipTrace | Novarm; mid-range desktop PCB tool | Proprietary/commercial | Traditional automation: schematic capture, component/footprint libraries, basic autorouter, DRC, 3D preview, manufacturing exports, batch output; limited BOM/library automation | Limited scripting and CI suitability; mostly GUI-centric workflow | Minimal AI/generative capability; weaker ecosystem/integration depth; less suited to large-team, high-constraint, or reproducible code-review workflows |
| EasyEDA / JLCPCB | EasyEDA + JLCPCB/LCSC cloud ecosystem; browser-based PCB CAD | Proprietary/cloud service with some free access | Strong practical automation for low-cost builds: browser CAD, integrated LCSC library/part search, BOM/CPL generation, direct JLCPCB ordering, online collaboration, basic autorouting/DRC, rapid component selection around in-stock catalog; adjacent AI-assisted part/design help is emerging in the ecosystem | Moderate for lightweight cloud collaboration, but weak for strict reproducible CI; exports available, yet workflow is platform-centric rather than code-native | Vendor lock-in to manufacturing ecosystem, limited advanced constraints, weaker scriptability/version control than KiCad/code-based stacks, and automation optimized for convenience over deep controllability (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 1-2, mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3) |


*Table: This table compares major schematic capture and PCB suites as of 2025, focusing on concrete automation and AI-adjacent capabilities, openness, and CI suitability. It is useful for distinguishing enterprise-optimized tools from open, scriptable workflows better suited to reproducible open-hardware development.*

### 2.1 KiCad and Its Scripting/IPC API

KiCad is the dominant open-source PCB design suite and serves as the de facto standard for open-hardware workflows. Its strengths lie in its Python scripting API, Action Plugin framework, command-line interface for Gerber/BOM generation, and text-based file formats amenable to Git version control. Research benchmarks frequently use KiCad as the standard format: Merrill (2021) developed an 11-design open benchmark suite in KiCad format for evaluating automated placement and routing, and Mamdouh et al. (2025) demonstrated automated Gerber and G-Code generation from KiCad, reporting a 13-fold improvement in routing efficiency compared to manual methods (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 1-2, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 89-94, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 94-101). KiCad integrates with external autorouters such as FreeRouting and supports ERC/DRC checking. However, it lacks native AI-driven placement or generative schematic capabilities; these must be added through external tools or plugins.

### 2.2 Altium Designer and Altium 365 / Co-Designer AI

Altium Designer is a widely used commercial PCB suite featuring ActiveBOM for supply-chain visibility, xSignals for high-speed design, and strong rules/constraint management. Altium 365 adds cloud-based collaboration, design review, and MCAD co-design. Altium has been positioning AI-assisted features through its Co-Designer and copilot-style tools focused on documentation, component search, and design assistance, though full autonomous generative placement and routing remain nascent. JITX and SnapMagic are noted as compatible with Altium's ecosystem, reducing migration friction for teams adopting AI-native tools (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3).

### 2.3 Cadence Allegro/OrCAD X and the Allegro AI/Optimality Engines

Cadence Allegro is an enterprise-grade PCB platform with a mature constraint-driven design methodology, signal/power integrity integration (via Sigrity), topology planning, and robust DFM/DRC. OrCAD X modernizes the interface while retaining Allegro's engine. Cadence has introduced AI/ML-powered optimization engines (marketed as Allegro X AI and Optimality Intelligence) that provide placement and routing guidance, congestion prediction, and productivity improvement. The broader Huang et al. (2021) survey documents how ML models—including CNNs for congestion/hotspot prediction and GNNs for netlists—are used across placement and routing stages, reflecting the direction Cadence and similar vendors are moving (huang2021machinelearningfor pages 33-34, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 9-11, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 30-33).

### 2.4 Siemens Xpedition / PADS, Zuken CR-8000, Autodesk Fusion Electronics, DipTrace, EasyEDA

**Siemens Xpedition/PADS** provides constraint-driven layout, advanced routing, HyperLynx SI/PI, and Valor-based DFM/manufacturing analysis. Automation is primarily rule- and constraint-driven rather than generative-AI-native. **Zuken CR-8000** excels at system-level and multi-board co-design with 3D integration, strong constraints, and enterprise library governance. **Autodesk Fusion Electronics** (successor to EAGLE) offers cloud-connected ECAD/MCAD co-design with push-and-shove routing and a basic autorouter, but limited AI features. **DipTrace** provides a mid-range traditional workflow with basic autorouting and DRC. **EasyEDA** is a browser-based PCB tool tightly integrated with the JLCPCB/LCSC manufacturing and component ecosystem, offering rapid part selection from in-stock catalogs, basic autorouting, and direct ordering—optimized for convenience over deep constraint management.

*Table: This table compares AI-native EDA startups and code-based/open-source PCB ecosystems by capability, automation method, CI suitability, and limitations. It is useful for identifying which tools are best suited for open-hardware, scriptable, and CI-driven workflows versus closed AI-assisted SaaS workflows.*

### 3.1 Flux.ai

Flux.ai is a cloud-based AI-assisted PCB design platform that leverages AI for real-time design validation, error detection and correction, design reuse, and team collaboration. The vendor claims up to 10× design acceleration (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3). Its browser-based collaborative workflow is attractive for distributed teams but offers less low-level scriptability than code-first alternatives.

### 3.2 JITX

JITX uses a proprietary code-driven design language with constraint solvers and generators to programmatically produce schematics and PCB layouts. It integrates supply-chain awareness to suggest component replacements and cost optimizations, and generates fault log files for troubleshooting. JITX is compatible with Altium, Cadence, and Zuken ecosystems (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3). Its code-native approach makes it well-suited for version control and CI, but its custom language creates a learning curve for non-programmers.

### 3.3 Quilter

Quilter is an RL-based automated PCB layout tool that has been used as an open-source baseline in academic benchmarks. Li et al. (2025) compared their HiePlace system against Quilter on academic PCB benchmarks (bm1–bm4), finding that HiePlace achieved 76.7%–91.3% cost reductions and runtime cuts of up to ~99% compared to Quilter, which had runtimes of 34–75 minutes per design (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 8-9). Quilter supports pre-placement constraint settings but is more suitable for smaller designs with fewer than approximately 100 components (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 8-9).

### 3.4 DeepPCB / InstaDeep

InstaDeep (now part of the BioNTech ecosystem) explored deep RL for PCB routing. Literature notes that AI-driven routing currently works well primarily on simpler 2-layer boards with fewer than 150 connection pairs (crocker2021physicallyconstrainedpcb pages 9-15). Li et al. (2023) at AAAI demonstrated FanoutNet, a deep RL method for PCB fanout automation using PPO with CNN and attention networks, achieving 100% routability on all industrial cases and 6.8% average wirelength improvement over state-of-the-art methods (li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2).

### 3.5 SnapMagic/SnapEDA

SnapMagic provides an AI-powered, conversational interface trained on component datasheets. It can autocomplete designs, recommend part substitutions based on local market availability and cost, and enact high-level requests such as "Add an MCU with 2 SPI ports and USB 2.0 and connect the USB port to a USB-C connector" (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 1-2, mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3). It integrates with mainstream EDA tools and excels at library, component, and BOM assistance, though it is not a full autonomous board synthesis tool.

### 3.6 CELUS, Diadem, Cofactr, Allspice

**CELUS Design Platform** takes a requirements-driven approach, enabling engineers to specify functional requirements that are then mapped to component selections and functional block/schematic generation, producing early BOM and architecture outputs. **Diadem** focuses on AI-assisted analog/mixed-signal circuit design optimization. **Cofactr** provides AI-driven supply-chain intelligence, procurement optimization, and inventory management as a complement to EDA tools. **Allspice** provides Git-based hardware version control with visual diff, design review portals, and CI hooks for EDA files—among the best-fitting tools for code-review-style hardware collaboration workflows.

### 3.7 Google/Microsoft ML Placement Efforts

Google's landmark work by Mirhoseini et al. (published in Nature, 2021) framed chip floorplanning as a deep RL problem and demonstrated that RL agents could generate placements comparable to or better than human experts for Google's tensor processing units. This work, while focused on VLSI/IC rather than PCB, has strongly influenced the broader field of automated placement research (crocker2021physicallyconstrainedpcb pages 9-15, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 3-5). The DREAMPlace framework treats placement as analogous to neural network training, achieving over 40× speedups on GPUs (huang2021machinelearningfor pages 30-33). Translation from chip floorplanning to manufacturable PCB layouts remains an open challenge.

---

## 4. Cloud/CI-Friendly and Code-Based Hardware Description Ecosystems

For open-hardware and CI-driven workflows, a critical requirement is that design files be text-based, scriptable, and version-controllable. Several ecosystems address this:

**atopile** is a Python-like hardware description language (HDL) for electronics with a pip-style package manager, enabling reusable circuit modules, dependency management, and netlist/BOM generation from code. Its text-first approach makes it excellent for Git workflows, CI pipelines, and reproducible builds, though its ecosystem is still maturing relative to GUI-based suites.

**tscircuit** brings electronic design into the TypeScript/React ecosystem, using npm-style packages and declarative/programmatic component definitions to generate schematics and PCB layouts. It is highly suited for software-engineer-friendly hardware development and CI integration.

**SKiDL** is a Python library that allows schematic capture and netlist generation entirely in code, with reusable circuit definitions and BOM generation. It is very scriptable and CI-friendly but requires downstream PCB layout tools (such as KiCad) for physical design.

**Horizon EDA** is a fully open-source EDA tool with a robust pool-based library management system. **gEDA** is a legacy open-source UNIX-style EDA suite with text/script-friendly formats. **Edea** is a Python library for programmatic manipulation of KiCad project files, useful as glue for CI pipelines, automated checks, and transformations. **PCBmodE** is a niche SVG- and Python-based PCB design tool offering high design programmability with source-text artifacts suitable for Git.

KiCad itself, combined with its Python scripting ecosystem, FreeRouting integration, and open file formats, remains the strongest mainstream option for open-hardware CI workflows. Research work has used KiCad as a recommended open benchmark format for PCB design evaluation (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 89-94, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 94-101).

---

## 5. Generative/Automation Capabilities and Limitations

### 5.1 Component Selection and BOM Optimization

SnapMagic and JITX provide the most mature AI-assisted component selection capabilities, with SnapMagic offering datasheet-trained recommendations and cost optimization based on market availability, and JITX integrating supply-chain awareness into its code-driven generators (mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 1-2, mamdouh2025afullyautomated pages 2-3). CELUS automates early-stage component selection from functional requirements. LLMs have also been explored for component selection, with generative AI capable of producing bill-of-materials from natural language specifications (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4).

### 5.2 Schematic Generation

LLMs have demonstrated a previously unknown capability for electronic circuit design from textual descriptions. Jansen (2023) introduced the Pins100 and MICRO25 benchmarks and showed that GPT-4 achieved 74% strict accuracy on component pinout generation (86% permissive) and between 60%–96% Pass@1 on generating full functioning devices including BOMs, schematics, and microcontroller code (jansen2023fromwordsto pages 4-5, jansen2023fromwordsto pages 1-2, jansen2023fromwordsto pages 3-4). However, complex open-ended designs still require moderate expert correction, and models tend to use obsolete parts and hallucinate libraries (jansen2023fromwordsto pages 7-9, jansen2023fromwordsto pages 9-10). LLMs can generate HDL code (Verilog) for digital circuits, with domain-specific fine-tuning improving results; the LLM4EDA survey documents applications in HDL generation, verification, and analysis across the EDA flow (zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 5-7, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 1-3, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 8-10). For analog IC design, generative AI methods including LLMs, GNNs, and VAEs have been applied to circuit sizing, with AnalogCoder automating 20 of 24 circuit tasks (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 1-2). Krishnamoorthy (2025) describes workflows where LLMs generate component-level schematics from circuit topology, with outputs that are editable and exportable to board layout tools (krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 1-2, krishnamoorthy2025leveraginggenerativeai pages 3-4).

### 5.3 Auto Placement

Reinforcement learning has emerged as the leading approach for automated PCB placement. Vassallo and Bajada (2024) demonstrated that RL agents trained with SAC and TD3 algorithms and adaptive reward signals achieved average post-routing wirelength reductions of 21% (SAC) and 17% (TD3) compared to simulated annealing baselines on unseen circuit layouts (vassallo2024learningcircuitplacement pages 5-6, vassallo2024learningcircuitplacement pages 1-2). Li et al. (2025) presented HiePlace, a hierarchical mathematical-programming approach achieving 66% cost reduction with 7.7× speedup on 19 PCB benchmarks, and 76.7%–91.3% cost reduction versus the RL-based Quilter tool on academic benchmarks (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 1-2, li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 11-12). At the IC level, DREAMPlace achieved over 40× GPU speedup for analytical placement (huang2021machinelearningfor pages 30-33).

### 5.4 Autorouting

FanoutNet (Li et al., 2023) applied deep RL (PPO with CNN and attention networks) to PCB fanout automation—a pre-routing stage—achieving 100% routability on all industrial test cases and 6.8% average wirelength improvement (li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2). Traditional approaches including A*-based rip-up-and-reroute remain common in practice, as demonstrated in the Merrill (2021) benchmark suite where the router prioritized connectivity and never produced logical-vs-schematic errors (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 85-89, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 89-94). AI-driven routing currently works best on simpler boards; production-scale complex multi-layer routing with differential pairs and impedance matching remains challenging for fully automated methods (crocker2021physicallyconstrainedpcb pages 9-15).

### 5.5 DFM/DRC

ML-based DFM and DRC checking is advancing through CNN-based hotspot detection, congestion prediction, and IR-drop estimation. The Huang et al. (2021) survey documents RouteNet for DRC/routability prediction, PowerNet/GridNet for power/EM prediction, and PRIMAL for RTL power estimation achieving 50× speedup with less than 5% error (huang2021machinelearningfor pages 33-34, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 9-11, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 30-33, huang2021machinelearningfor pages 42-44). Siemens' Valor ecosystem and Cadence's Sigrity tools incorporate manufacturing analysis, though these are primarily rule- and simulation-driven rather than generative-AI-based.

### 5.6 Key Limitations

Across all tools and methods, several cross-cutting limitations persist:

- **Manufacturability**: AI-generated placements and routes may not respect the full set of manufacturing constraints (impedance, thermal, creepage) that production boards require. Routability is often modeled via proxies like wirelength and net crossings rather than comprehensive DFM rules (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 11-12).
- **Editability and controllability**: LLM-generated designs often require moderate expert correction, and proprietary AI tools may produce outputs that are difficult to manually refine or override (jansen2023fromwordsto pages 7-9, jansen2023fromwordsto pages 9-10).
- **Scalability**: RL-based placement methods struggle with large industrial boards (>100–200 components), and LLM-based approaches face context-window and consistency limitations for complex multi-stage designs (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 8-9, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 3-5).
- **Verification**: The lack of electronic simulators with large component libraries blocks automated end-to-end testing of generated designs; manual verification remains necessary for safety-critical applications (jansen2023fromwordsto pages 7-9, jansen2023fromwordsto pages 9-10).
- **Version control and CI**: Binary file formats in commercial tools (Altium, Cadence, Siemens) hinder Git-based workflows; only open-source tools (KiCad, atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL) and dedicated platforms (Allspice) provide first-class CI support.

| Zhong et al. (LLM4EDA, arXiv) | 2024 | LLMs for EDA | Survey of LLM chatbots, HDL/script generation, verification/analysis | Survey of recent LLM4EDA systems and datasets | Concludes LLMs are effective for HDL generation, chatbots, bug summarization/repair, and assertion generation; highlights pass@k-style evaluation such as VerilogEval for generated RTL (zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 5-7, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 1-3, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 8-10) | Physical design remains difficult at full scale; hallucination, evaluation, and integration with downstream toolchains remain open problems (zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 5-7, zhong2024llm4edaemergingprogress pages 3-5) |
| Zadeh & Elamien (IEEE Access) | 2025 | Generative AI for analog IC design | Survey of GNNs, LLMs, VAEs, Bayesian optimization hybrids | Analog circuit sizing and schematic/layout-aware tasks | Reports AnalogCoder automated 20/24 circuits; summarizes reduced iterations and faster execution in LLM-assisted analog design pipelines (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 1-2) | LLMs still struggle with precise numerical reasoning, parasitic/PVT handling, and unseen topologies; many studies remain limited to simpler circuits (zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 12-13, zadeh2025generativeaifor pages 1-2) |


*Table: This table summarizes key benchmark and case-study results from 2021-2025 literature on AI/ML-assisted PCB and EDA automation. It highlights where measurable gains have been reported and where current methods still face limits in scale, verification, and manufacturability.*

---

## 7. Recommendations for Open-Hardware, CI-Driven Workflows

For teams prioritizing open-source, scriptable, CI-driven workflows, the most suitable stack combines:

1. **KiCad** as the primary schematic/PCB design tool, leveraging its Python scripting API, text-based file formats, and open benchmark ecosystem (merrill2021hungryforfully pages 89-94, merrill2021hungryforfully pages 94-101).
2. **Code-based HDLs** such as **atopile**, **tscircuit**, or **SKiDL** for programmatic circuit definition, reuse, and Git-native version control.
3. **Edea** for Python-based automation and manipulation of KiCad files in CI pipelines.
4. **Allspice** for Git-based hardware review, visual diff, and CI hooks.
5. **SnapMagic** or **JITX** for AI-assisted component selection and BOM optimization, with outputs compatible with KiCad/Altium/Cadence.
6. External autorouters (FreeRouting, or emerging RL-based tools) for routing automation, with manual review for production boards.

This stack preserves full traceability, editability, and reproducibility while incorporating AI assistance where it provides the most value—component selection, early schematic generation, and BOM optimization—while keeping human engineers in control of final placement, routing, and manufacturing preparation.

---

## 8. Conclusion

The EDA landscape in 2025 is characterized by a convergence of traditional constraint-driven automation with emerging ML/AI capabilities. Reinforcement learning has demonstrated measurable improvements in PCB placement (17–21% wirelength reduction) and fanout automation (100% routability with 6.8% wirelength gains) (vassallo2024learningcircuitplacement pages 5-6, li2023fanoutnetaneuralized pages 1-2, vassallo2024learningcircuitplacement pages 1-2). LLMs can generate functioning circuit designs from natural language with 60–96% Pass@1 on constrained benchmarks (jansen2023fromwordsto pages 1-2), and hierarchical mathematical approaches like HiePlace dramatically outperform earlier RL tools on placement cost and runtime (li2025hieplaceefficienthierarchical pages 9-11). However, full end-to-end automated PCB design—from requirements through manufacturable Gerber files—remains an open challenge. The most practical near-term approach combines open, scriptable tools for reproducibility and CI with targeted AI assistance for component selection, early design exploration, and validation, while maintaining human oversight for final design decisions and manufacturing preparation.

| Tool Name | Vendor/Type | License/Open-Source | AI/Automation Features (component selection, schematic generation, auto-placement, autorouting, DFM/DRC, BOM/supply chain) | Scriptability/CI Suitability | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| KiCad | KiCad project; desktop schematic/PCB suite | Open-source (GPL) | Strong automation but little built-in AI: Python scripting/plugins, Action Plugins, CLI exporters, IPC/API ecosystem, netlist/BOM generation, ERC/DRC, push-and-shove router, external autorouting via FreeRouting, library-table driven reuse; widely used as open benchmark format and automation backbone in research workflows (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Excellent for open-hardware CI: text-based files, Python automation, BOM/Gerber generation in pipelines, good Git friendliness; strongest fit for reproducible/open workflows | No first-party generative schematic or placement AI; FreeRouting integration is external and limited; complex constraint management and enterprise PLM/supply-chain tooling lag top commercial suites |
| Altium Designer / Altium 365 | Altium; enterprise PCB suite + cloud collaboration | Proprietary/commercial | Mature automation stack: ActiveBOM and supply-chain visibility, rules/constraints, interactive routing, xSignals/high-speed checks, cloud collaboration/review, MCAD co-design; emerging AI/copilot-style assistance in Altium 365/CoDesigner positioning around search, documentation and design assistance rather than fully automatic PCB generation | Moderate: scripts/APIs and cloud workflows exist, but CI is less open than code-first tools; version control better in Altium 365 than classic binary-file workflows | Proprietary stack; editability/version diffs weaker than plain-text/open formats; AI features are assistive and workflow-centric, not fully controllable generative placement/routing |
| Cadence Allegro / OrCAD X | Cadence; high-end PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Very strong automation: constraint-driven design, signal/power integrity integration, Allegro routing engines, topology planning, DFM/DRC, library/data management; AI/ML positioning via Allegro X AI/optimization engines for placement/routing guidance and productivity; strong manufacturing and enterprise flows | Good enterprise automation through Tcl/SKILL/APIs and batch flows, but less Git/CI-native than text/code-based ecosystems | Expensive and complex; strong automation still requires expert constraint setup; closed formats and licensing make open-hardware/CI workflows harder than KiCad/SKiDL-style stacks |
| Siemens Xpedition / PADS Professional | Siemens EDA; enterprise PCB suite | Proprietary/commercial | Constraint-driven layout, advanced routing, HyperLynx SI/PI analysis, DFM/manufacturing links, enterprise library management; strong automation in rule checking, design reuse, and verification; Siemens portfolio also connects to Valor-style manufacturing analysis in broader flow | Enterprise scripting/integration exists, but cloud-native CI friendliness is limited relative to open text-first tools | Powerful but heavyweight; AI is mostly optimization/analysis assist rather than transparent generative design; high barrier for small teams and open-hardware collaboration |
| Zuken CR-8000 | Zuken; enterprise system-level and PCB platform | Proprietary/commercial | Strong system/board co-design, 3D multi-board, harness/backplane integration, rule-driven placement and routing, advanced constraints, DFM/DRC/manufacturing preparation, parts/library governance; automation is rule-centric more than LLM/generative | Enterprise integrations exist, but not especially Git/CI-native; better suited to controlled corporate flows | Closed ecosystem; steep learning curve; limited visibility/editability for code-review-style hardware workflows; AI-native features less prominent than in newer startup tools |
| Autodesk Fusion Electronics / EAGLE | Autodesk; integrated ECAD/MCAD, cloud-connected | Proprietary/commercial (free tiers historically limited) | Good mainstream automation: schematic capture, push-and-shove routing, autorouter, MCAD integration in Fusion, library/BOM generation, cloud project sharing; useful for mechanical-electrical co-design more than AI-native generation | Some automation/export possible, but weaker scripting and CI ergonomics than KiCad/code-first stacks | EAGLE-to-Fusion transition created ecosystem uncertainty; less capable for advanced constraints than Cadence/Altium; limited built-in AI and weaker open workflow support |
| DipTrace | Novarm; mid-range desktop PCB tool | Proprietary/commercial | Traditional automation: schematic capture, component/footprint libraries, basic autorouter, DRC, 3D preview, manufacturing exports, batch output; limited BOM/library automation | Limited scripting and CI suitability; mostly GUI-centric workflow | Minimal AI/generative capability; weaker ecosystem/integration depth; less suited to large-team, high-constraint, or reproducible code-review workflows |
| EasyEDA / JLCPCB | EasyEDA + JLCPCB/LCSC cloud ecosystem; browser-based PCB CAD | Proprietary/cloud service with some free access | Strong practical automation for low-cost builds: browser CAD, integrated LCSC library/part search, BOM/CPL generation, direct JLCPCB ordering, online collaboration, basic autorouting/DRC, rapid component selection around in-stock catalog; adjacent AI-assisted part/design help is emerging in the ecosystem | Moderate for lightweight cloud collaboration, but weak for strict reproducible CI; exports available, yet workflow is platform-centric rather than code-native | Vendor lock-in to manufacturing ecosystem, limited advanced constraints, weaker scriptability/version control than KiCad/code-based stacks, and automation optimized for convenience over deep controllability (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000008) |

| Edea | Open-source utility | Open-source | Python manipulation/automation of KiCad project files and workflows | No native AI; automation via Python APIs | High: very suitable as glue for CI, checks, transformations, and code generation around KiCad | Not a full CAD front-end; depends on KiCad and surrounding tooling |
| PCBmodE | Code-based / open-source | Open-source | SVG- and Python-based PCB generation with high design programmability | No native AI; graphics/code-driven generation | High: source-text artifacts work well with Git and reproducible builds | Niche workflow; weaker support for advanced mainstream CAD constraints and enterprise flows |
| KiCad + scripting ecosystem (reference open workflow) | Open-source baseline | Open-source | Full schematic/PCB CAD, Python scripting, file-format automation, Gerber generation, third-party autorouting integration | Conventional automation via scripts/plugins rather than native GenAI; used in automated benchmark/manufacturing workflows (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Very High: among the best mainstream options for open hardware + CI because files, plugins, and automation are accessible | Native AI is limited; advanced autorouting/constraint automation trails top commercial suites; external tools often needed for full automation (pqac-00000007, pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) |


*Table: This table compares AI-native EDA startups and code-based/open-source PCB ecosystems by capability, automation method, CI suitability, and limitations. It is useful for identifying which tools are best suited for open-hardware, scriptable, and CI-driven workflows versus closed AI-assisted SaaS workflows.*
artifacts.artifact-02:
| Paper/Tool | Year | Task | Method | Benchmark/Dataset | Key Quantitative Result | Limitations/Notes |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vassallo & Bajada (DATE) | 2024 | PCB placement | Reinforcement learning (TD3, SAC) with adaptive rewards | Unseen circuit layouts; compared against simulated-annealing PCB baseline | 17% (TD3) and 21% (SAC) average reduction in post-routing wirelength vs simulated annealing; faster convergence also reported (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) | Placement only; benchmark is limited relative to full industrial PCB flows; routing/manufacturability still downstream concerns (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000002) |
| Li et al. (FanoutNet, AAAI) | 2023 | PCB fanout automation | Deep RL using PPO + CNN + attention-based policy/value networks | Real-world industrial PCB benchmarks | 100% routability in all industrial cases; 6.8% average wirelength improvement vs prior methods (pqac-00000001) | Focused on fanout/pre-routing rather than full end-to-end PCB layout; depends on downstream router and industrial constraints encoding (pqac-00000001) |
| Li et al. (HiePlace, IEEE TCAD) | 2025 | Hierarchical PCB placement | Mathematical programming + dynamic prioritization + relaxation | 19 PCB benchmarks (15 industrial, 4 academic); Quilter comparison on academic cases | 66% cost reduction and 7.7× speedup overall; vs Quilter, 76.7%–91.3% cost reduction and runtime cuts up to ~99%+ on academic benchmarks (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000020, pqac-00000022) | Not an LLM/generative method; routability modeled via proxies; double-sided layouts noted as future work (pqac-00000022) |
| Jansen (Words to Wires, arXiv) | 2023 | Natural-language-to-circuit generation | GPT-4 / Claude-class LLMs generating BOMs, pinouts, schematics/netlists, and code | Pins100 (100 components), MICRO25 (25 Arduino-style device tasks) | GPT-4 reached 74% strict and 86% permissive pinout accuracy on Pins100; reported 60%–96% Pass@1 on full-device generation across benchmarks/cases (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000026) | Open-ended designs still required moderate expert correction; obsolete parts, hallucinated libraries, and lack of end-to-end simulation remain issues (pqac-00000025, pqac-00000027) |
| Merrill (thesis) | 2021 | Fully automated embedded PCB design | Simulated annealing placement + A*-based rip-up-and-reroute | 11 open PCB benchmarks in KiCad format | Demonstrated full automatic placement/routing workflow and benchmark suite; router prioritized connectivity and reportedly never produced LvS errors in cited evaluation description (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) | Hard boards still challenged automation; quantitative summary is less standardized than later ML papers; many advanced constraints omitted from benchmarks (pqac-00000019, pqac-00000021) |
| Huang et al. (ACM TODAES survey) | 2021 | ML for EDA survey | Survey of RL, GNN, CNN, XGBoost, SVM, GAN, DRL, etc. | Cross-stage review: HLS, logic synthesis, placement/routing, verification, manufacturing | DREAMPlace reported >40× GPU speedup for placement; PRIMAL reported 50× speedup with <5% error for RTL power estimation; GRANNITE reported ~2 orders-of-magnitude runtime speedup with ~5.5% error (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000032) | Survey spans mostly IC/VLSI and broader EDA, not only PCB; results are drawn from multiple subfields and are not directly comparable head-to-head (pqac-00000028, pqac-00000029, pqac-00000032) |
| Zhong et al. (LLM4EDA, arXiv) | 2024 | LLMs for EDA | Survey of LLM chatbots, HDL/script generation, verification/analysis | Survey of recent LLM4EDA systems and datasets | Concludes LLMs are effective for HDL generation, chatbots, bug summarization/repair, and assertion generation; highlights pass@k-style evaluation such as VerilogEval for generated RTL (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000011, pqac-00000014, pqac-00000035) | Physical design remains difficult at full scale; hallucination, evaluation, and integration with downstream toolchains remain open problems (pqac-00000009, pqac-00000016) |
| Zadeh & Elamien (IEEE Access) | 2025 | Generative AI for analog IC design | Survey of GNNs, LLMs, VAEs, Bayesian optimization hybrids | Analog circuit sizing and schematic/layout-aware tasks | Reports AnalogCoder automated 20/24 circuits; summarizes reduced iterations and faster execution in LLM-assisted analog design pipelines (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000013) | LLMs still struggle with precise numerical reasoning, parasitic/PVT handling, and unseen topologies; many studies remain limited to simpler circuits (pqac-00000010, pqac-00000013) |


*Table: This table summarizes key benchmark and case-study results from 2021-2025 literature on AI/ML-assisted PCB and EDA automation. It highlights where measurable gains have been reported and where current methods still face limits in scale, verification, and manufacturability.*

info.query: Provide a state-of-the-art landscape of generative, AI-assisted, and automated tools for electronic design automation (EDA), schematic capture, and printed circuit board (PCB) design as of 2025. Cover: (a) mainstream schematic/PCB design suites and their automation/AI features (KiCad and its scripting/IPC API, Altium Designer and Altium 365 / Co-Designer AI, Cadence Allegro/OrCAD X and the Allegro AI/Optimality engines, Siemens Xpedition / PADS, Zuken CR-8000, Autodesk Fusion Electronics / EAGLE, DipTrace, EasyEDA); (b) AI-native and generative EDA startups and products (Flux.ai, JITX, Quilter, DeepPCB / InstaDeep, Celus, CELUS Design Platform, Diadem, Cofactr, Allspice, Snapmagic/SnapEDA, Spectra/Untether, Microsoft/Google ML-placement efforts); (c) cloud/CI-friendly and code-based hardware description ecosystems (atopile, tscircuit, SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA, Edea, Horizon EDA); and (d) for each, the concrete generative/automation capability offered (component selection, schematic generation, auto placement, autorouting, DFM/DRC, BOM and supply-chain optimization) and its limitations (manufacturability, editability, controllability, scriptability for version control / CI). Quote benchmark or case-study results where available. Emphasize which tools are open-source or scriptable and therefore suitable for an open-hardware, CI-driven workflow. Cite vendor pages and ~5-8 recent (2021-2025) peer-reviewed or arXiv papers.
